qa.evidence_quote: To supply the global aviation industry at current levels of consumption would require some 274.8 million acres of cropland... Clearly the use of biofuels would detract from food production and there is little argument for using biofuels as a major replacement for fossil fuels.

qa.question: Why does the Honda Insight count only as a partial zero-emission vehicle?
qa.answer: The Insight has an engine and a battery-driven motor. In heavy slow traffic it runs from the battery and emits nothing locally, behaving like a pure EV. At higher loads the petrol engine fires up, so it still emits exhaust gases — it is therefore only partly emission-free.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: In heavy traffic the car is driven from the batteries via the motor/generator only. Hence it can be classified as a partial zero-emission vehicle (PZEV).

qa.question: Why are globally distributed solar farms with HV DC links seen as attractive?
qa.answer: If solar farms are spread across longitudes and joined by high-voltage DC transmission, sunshine somewhere on the network is producing power at any hour of day. That smooths the supply, removing the day-and-night problem of a single site and giving steady electricity for EVs around the clock.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)
source.chapter: 13. Power Generation for Transport, Particularly for Zero Emissions

qa.evidence_quote: An attractive option is to use a series of solar farms distributed around the globe and connected by high-voltage, high-efficiency electric transmission lines. Global distribution would provide solar electricity throughout the day and night.

qa.question: Why is undersea current energy more predictable than wind or solar?
qa.answer: Ocean currents are driven by tides and large-scale circulation, which follow regular astronomical and oceanographic patterns. Their speed and direction can be forecast accurately, while wind and sunshine depend on weather. That predictability makes underwater turbines a steady source of energy for a power grid.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)
source.chapter: 13. Power Generation for Transport, Particularly for Zero Emissions

qa.evidence_quote: Marine currents are more predictable than wind and solar.

qa.question: Why did CARB switch to a credit-based system for zero-emission vehicles?
qa.answer: Strict ZEV-only rules ignored the fact that some hybrids may pollute less than a battery car charged from a coal-fired grid. A credit system lets a car maker earn fractions of a ZEV credit for selling cleaner-than-average vehicles, which better reflects true well-to-wheels emissions.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: Vehicles that are not fully 'zero emission' might actually produce fewer emissions in the whole well-to-wheel analysis than a zero-emission vehicle (ZEV)... The regulations now incorporate a system of credits

qa.question: Why is a purpose-built EV better than converting a petrol car?
qa.answer: A car designed from scratch as an EV, like the GM EV1, can use slippery aerodynamics, low-rolling-resistance tyres and battery placement made specifically for an electric drive. A converted petrol car carries spare weight and shape, so it ends up with less range for the same battery size.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: The advanced performance clearly illustrates the benefits of designing the vehicle as an electric car rather than simply converting an existing vehicle.

qa.question: Why is the market for Low-Speed Vehicles expected to grow in western countries?
qa.answer: Western countries have ageing populations. Many active retired people still want to visit friends, shop and run errands, but do not need to drive long distances or at motorway speeds. Quiet, simple, short-range electric LSVs match this lifestyle, so demand is likely to rise steadily.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: These vehicles are particularly targeted at fairly active retired people who still want to get about... The demographics of most western countries show there is little doubt that the market for this type of vehicle will grow steadily.

qa.question: How does the Toyota Prius split power between its engine, motor, generator and wheels?
qa.answer: The Prius uses a planetary gearset called the power splitter and an electronically controlled CVT. The split sends engine power partly to the wheels and partly to a generator that charges the battery and feeds the motor. A dashboard display shows energy flow at every moment in real time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: The transmission incorporates a fairly complex system of planetary gears, called a 'power splitter', which directs power between the IC engine, the electric motor, the generator and the wheels

qa.question: How does the Chevrolet Volt switch from pure-electric to series-hybrid driving?
qa.answer: The Volt runs as a pure EV from its lithium battery for the first 40 to 80 km. Once the battery falls to a preset low level, the petrol engine starts but only drives a generator, which feeds the traction motor — making the Volt operate as a series hybrid.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: The car runs entirely from the battery for the first 25–50 miles (40–80 km), after which it functions primarily as a series hybrid with the IC engine driving the generator to power the traction electric motor.

qa.question: How does the Honda Insight recover energy when the driver brakes?
qa.answer: On a light brake press, the Insight's motor/generator switches into generator mode. It loads the wheels and slows the car, turning kinetic energy back into electricity stored in the battery. Only when the driver presses harder do the conventional friction brakes engage to bring the car to a stop.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)

qa.evidence_quote: When the brake pedal is pressed lightly, the Insight's electric motor operates in regeneration mode, and the car begins to slow just as it would with normal brakes. Once the brake pedal is pressed further, the normal brakes come into play

qa.question: How much of Britain's electric-transport demand could the Severn Barrage cover?
qa.answer: A Severn Barrage near Weston-super-Mare would generate 2 GW continuously. A larger dam further east would double that and yield about 36.5 TWh per year — roughly 15% of all the electricity Britain would need to electrify its transport, from a single tidal site.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)
source.chapter: 13. Power Generation for Transport, Particularly for Zero Emissions

qa.evidence_quote: a dam further east would produce double this amount, that is 36.5 TWh of electricity per annum, 15% of the requirement for electric transport in Britain.

qa.question: What efficiency gap separates old coal stations from modern combined-cycle stations?
qa.answer: Old coal-fired power stations averaged around 28% efficiency — only about a quarter of the fuel's energy reached the grid. Modern combined-cycle stations, which use two heat engines in tandem, regularly exceed 50%, with the best designs reaching 60% — roughly doubling the useful output per unit of fuel.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Technology Explained, 2nd Ed. (Larminie & Lowry, Wiley)
source.chapter: 13. Power Generation for Transport, Particularly for Zero Emissions

qa.evidence_quote: Combined cycle power stations have achieved efficiencies of more than 60% (electrical energy generated/energy in the fuel). In general service combined cycle efficiencies are over 50%

qa.question: What is a Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV)?
qa.answer: A Hybrid Electric Vehicle combines an internal combustion engine and an electric motor within the same drive train. The electric motor usually supports the engine to improve fuel economy or performance, and the vehicle is propelled by either the engine or the electric drive.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: A Hybrid Electric Vehicle (HEV) combines an ICE and an electric motor within the drive train.

qa.question: What distinguishes a Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle from a regular hybrid?
qa.answer: A Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle carries a larger battery than a normal hybrid and can be recharged from home outlets or charging stations. Many PHEVs can run in fully electric mode if enough energy is stored in the battery, unlike standard hybrids.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: a vehicle equipped, in general, with a larger battery compared to HEVs, that allows recharging of the battery via home outlets or at charging stations

qa.question: Why are Full Electric Vehicles not truly zero-emission?
qa.answer: While Full Electric Vehicles release no pollutants while driving, the electricity used to recharge their batteries usually comes from power plants that do emit pollutants. So overall emissions depend on the cleanliness of the country's power generation, not just the vehicle itself.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: the recharging of the batteries will cause emissions depending on the actual emissions of the power generation in the country

qa.question: What is range anxiety in the context of electric vehicles?
qa.answer: Range anxiety refers to driver worry about an EV's limited range plus its unpredictable depletion when heating, air conditioning, or detours are added. Because EV ranges below 300 km in good conditions are common, drivers fear running out of charge before reaching their destination.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The latter two issues are often grouped together as one and discussed under the title of range anxiety

qa.question: Why has the cost of batteries been such a big obstacle to EV adoption?
qa.answer: Batteries make up a major portion of an EV's total price, keeping vehicles expensive even with government subsidies. Some companies have tried leasing batteries to customers to offset this cost. Forecasts suggest battery costs will fall dramatically in coming years, easing the pressure.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: A major factor in the cost of such vehicles is the cost of the battery

qa.question: What is regenerative braking in an electric machine?
qa.answer: Regenerative braking is the braking mode in an electric machine where, instead of wasting kinetic energy as heat, the machine converts the vehicle's mechanical energy from the wheels back into electrical energy. This recovered energy can recharge the battery and help extend driving range.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The braking mode in electric machines is referred to as regenerative braking.

qa.question: Why does an electric motor typically give a vehicle better acceleration than an internal combustion engine?
qa.answer: Electric motors deliver full torque even at very low speeds, and their instantaneous power can be two or three times their rated power. This means an electric drive gives strong launch and excellent acceleration from a relatively small, nominally rated motor.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: the motor provides full torque at low speeds and the instantaneous power rating can be two or three times the rated power of the motor

qa.question: Why have DC series motors become obsolete in modern EV applications?
qa.answer: Although DC series motors matched road load characteristics well and were easy to control in older prototype EVs, their large physical size and heavy maintenance requirements (mostly due to brushes and commutators) have made them obsolete in the automotive industry and in motor drives generally.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: the size and maintenance requirements of DC motors are making their use obsolete not just in the automotive industry

qa.question: What is a brushless DC motor?
qa.answer: A brushless DC motor is a permanent magnet motor with concentrated three-phase windings whose air-gap flux distribution is trapezoidal. It is driven by a six-switch inverter, much like an induction motor, but its control is simpler because of the trapezoidal back-EMF shape.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The trapezoidal motors have concentrated three-phase windings and are also known as brushless DC motors.

qa.question: How do switched reluctance motors produce torque differently from DC or PM motors?
qa.answer: DC and permanent magnet motors produce torque by mutual interaction between two orthogonal magnetic fields. Switched reluctance motors instead use a varying-reluctance flux path: the rotor is pulled toward whichever position minimises the magnetic circuit's reluctance, giving torque without rotor windings or magnets.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: by using a varying reluctance flux path where the rotor moves toward attaining the minimum reluctance position

qa.question: Why are switched reluctance motors attractive for vehicle traction despite their drawbacks?
qa.answer: SR motors have a simple rotor with no windings, magnets, or cage, which gives them excellent fault tolerance, a high torque-to-inertia ratio, and the widest possible constant-power speed range. These traits are ideal for traction, though acoustic noise and torque ripple remain known concerns.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The constant power speed range is the widest possible in SR motors compared to other technologies, which makes it ideally suitable for traction applications.

qa.question: In an electric machine, what is the back-EMF?
qa.answer: The back-EMF, also called motional voltage, is the voltage induced in the armature when its conductors move through the magnetic field. For a DC machine, back-EMF is proportional to rotor speed when the field flux is held constant by design.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Ea is the induced voltage or motional voltage, more commonly known as back-emf of the machine

qa.question: How is a separately excited DC motor defined in the CRC reference?
qa.answer: A separately excited DC motor is one whose armature and field windings are supplied by independently controlled DC sources. This independence allows separate control of armature current and field current, giving the maximum flexibility for tuning torque and speed in the machine.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: When the armature and field windings are supplied from independently controlled DC sources, then it is known as a separately excited DC machine.

qa.question: Why is a DC series motor good for fast starting acceleration?
qa.answer: In a series DC machine the armature and field windings are in series, so the same current passes through both. This produces a very high starting torque, which translates into rapid acceleration. The trade-off is the loss of independent control over flux and armature current.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The greatest advantage of the series machine is the very high starting torque that helps achieve rapid acceleration.

qa.question: What does it mean to minimise losses in a separately excited DC motor?
qa.answer: Losses in a separately excited DC motor are minimised when the armature-circuit losses (mainly resistive copper losses in the armature) equal the field-circuit losses. The controller can pick armature voltage and field flux to balance those two loss terms, maximising efficiency at the chosen operating point.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The loss in DC machines is minimized when armature circuit-dependent losses equal the field circuit dependent losses

qa.question: Why does moving the armature winding into the stator simplify AC machines?
qa.answer: In AC machines the armature circuit sits in the stationary stator instead of the rotor, so commutators and brushes (needed in DC machines to flip rotor current) are no longer required. This eliminates brush wear and the electromagnetic interference associated with mechanical commutation.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The advantage gained in having the armature circuit in the stator is the elimination of the commutator and brushes of the DC machines.

qa.question: What is synchronous speed in a three-phase AC machine?
qa.answer: Synchronous speed is the rotational speed at which the stator's three-phase mmf wave rotates around the air gap. It equals the angular frequency of the applied stator voltage, and the resulting mmf has a constant peak amplitude that circles the stator at this speed.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.section: 6.3.4.3 Resultant mmf in a Balanced System

qa.evidence_quote: rotates around the stator circumference at a constant speed equal to the angular speed of the applied stator voltages. This speed is known as the synchronous speed.

qa.question: What is a squirrel-cage induction motor?
qa.answer: A squirrel-cage induction motor has a rotor made of short-circuited copper or aluminium bars set axially around stacked laminations, connected at each end by end rings shaped like a squirrel cage. Its rugged construction and low cost make it the workhorse of industry.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The rotor windings consist of short-circuited copper bars that form the shape of a squirrel cage.

qa.question: What is slip in an induction motor?
qa.answer: Slip is the difference between the rotor speed and the stator's synchronous speed, expressed as a fraction of the synchronous speed. The rotor must spin slightly slower than the field for the rotor bars to cut flux, induce a voltage, and produce torque.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The slip speed expressed as a fraction of the synchronous speed is known as the slip

qa.question: Why is an induction motor usually run at small slip values?
qa.answer: Operating at small slip keeps the rotor frequency low, so the rotor leakage reactance has little effect and the motor stays in the linear part of its torque-speed curve. This avoids the heavy losses, voltage drops, and heating that come with high-slip operation.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: operating the machine at small slip values improves the efficiency

qa.question: How much vehicle range can regenerative braking typically add?
qa.answer: Regenerative braking can extend the driving range of an electric vehicle by roughly 10 to 15 percent. Not all of the kinetic energy is captured because sudden stops would demand power far higher than the propulsion motor's rating, so mechanical brakes are still required.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Regenerative braking can increase the range of electric vehicle by a small percentage (about 10%–15%).

qa.question: When does an induction machine work as a generator during braking?
qa.answer: An induction machine acts as a generator when its rotor turns faster than the synchronous speed, giving it a negative slip. The induced rotor currents reverse polarity and the electromagnetic torque becomes negative, opposing rotation and decelerating the vehicle while feeding energy back.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The induction machine works as a generator when it is operated with a negative slip

qa.question: What is a four-quadrant drive in EV motor control?
qa.answer: A four-quadrant drive is a motor controller that can deliver positive or negative torque at positive or negative shaft speed. This lets the same machine run forward or in reverse and switch smoothly between motoring and regenerative braking modes, which EVs and HEVs always require.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: the electric motor is controlled by the drive to deliver positive or negative torque at positive or negative speed

qa.question: Why do permanent magnet machines avoid the rotor copper losses found in induction motors?
qa.answer: In a PM machine the rotor field comes from permanent magnets rather than from coil currents. Because no rotor windings need to be energised, there is no resistive (copper) loss on the rotor, and there is no separate field exciter circuit to maintain.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: This configuration eliminates the rotor copper loss as well as the need for maintenance of the field exciting circuit.

qa.question: What are the three common types of magnets used in PM machines?
qa.answer: Permanent magnet motors typically use ferrites, samarium cobalt, or neodymium-iron-boron magnets. Ferrites are cheap but weak; samarium cobalt gives much higher flux density at higher cost; neodymium-iron-boron offers the strongest residual flux density (1.1 to 1.25 tesla) but is sensitive to temperature.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The common types of magnets used in PM machines are the ferrites, samarium cobalt (SmCo), and neodymium-iron-boron (NdFeB).

qa.question: Why is the interior PM machine well suited to EV applications needing flux weakening?
qa.answer: In an interior PMSM the magnets are buried inside the rotor, so the quadrature-axis inductance can be much larger than the direct-axis inductance. This larger difference enables flux-weakening operation, giving a wide constant-power speed range and reducing the need for multiple transmission gears.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The larger difference in the d and q-axes inductances make the interior PM more suitable for flux weakening operation

qa.question: What distinguishes a PM brushless DC motor from a PMSM?
qa.answer: PM brushless DC motors have trapezoidal back-EMF waveforms produced by concentrated stator windings, while PMSMs use sinusoidally distributed windings and have sinusoidal back-EMFs. The BLDC motor only needs six discrete rotor positions per electrical revolution, simplifying its control compared to the PMSM.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The trapezoidal-shaped back-emf waveforms in these machines are due to the concentrated windings of the machine used instead of the sinusoidally distributed windings used in the PMSMs.

qa.question: Why do PMSMs require a high-resolution position sensor?
qa.answer: A PMSM produces smooth torque by precisely shaping the stator currents so they stay aligned with the rotor magnet field. That alignment requires accurate knowledge of rotor angle, so a high-resolution position sensor (and current sensors) feed information back to the digital controller.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The smooth torque output is maintained in these machines by shaping the motor currents, which requires a high-resolution position sensor and current sensors.

qa.question: What are the three regions of an SRM's torque-speed envelope?
qa.answer: An SRM drive has a constant torque region below the base speed (current and torque limited), a constant power region where conduction angle is increased as speed rises, and a high-speed natural region where torque falls roughly as one over speed squared.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: The torque–speed plane of an SRM drive can be divided into three regions

qa.question: What is aerodynamic drag on a vehicle composed of?
qa.answer: Aerodynamic drag on a vehicle is made of two parts: shape drag, due to high-pressure zones in front and low-pressure zones behind the body, and skin friction, caused by shear stress in the boundary layer. Shape drag accounts for over 90 percent of total drag.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 2. Fundamentals of Conventional Vehicles and Powertrains

qa.evidence_quote: The external aerodynamic resistance is comprised of two components, shape drag and skin friction.

qa.question: Why does a rolling tyre create a retarding force on a moving vehicle?
qa.answer: When a tyre rolls, hysteresis in the rubber makes the contact patch's normal pressure higher in front and lower behind. That shifts the road's reaction force ahead of the wheel centre, creating a moment that opposes the wheel's motion - the rolling resistance.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 2. Fundamentals of Conventional Vehicles and Powertrains

qa.evidence_quote: The normal force due to the road is shifted from the center of the tire in the direction of motion. This shift produces a moment that exerts a retarding torque on the wheel.

qa.question: What is gradeability for a vehicle?
qa.answer: Gradeability is the maximum slope that a vehicle can start climbing from a standstill while all of its wheels remain on the gradient. It depends on the available tractive effort versus the combined resistance forces acting on the vehicle at start-up.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 2. Fundamentals of Conventional Vehicles and Powertrains

qa.evidence_quote: The gradeability of a vehicle is the maximum gradient on which the vehicle can start climbing from stand-still with all the wheels of the vehicle on the gradient at the time of start.

qa.question: Why is proper brake balance important when designing a regenerative braking system?
qa.answer: Maximum braking deceleration is only achieved when all four tyres reach the peak friction level at the same time. If the front or rear axle locks up first, those wheels lose cornering grip, the vehicle becomes unstable, and the full braking potential is wasted.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 2. Fundamentals of Conventional Vehicles and Powertrains

qa.evidence_quote: the vehicle will not achieve the maximum braking deceleration unless all four tires are brought to the peak friction level simultaneously

qa.question: How much of total braking force may sit on the front axle in a high-performance car?
qa.answer: In high-performance vehicles, as much as 80 percent of the braking force can act on the front axle. This concentration on the front wheels creates a much larger opportunity to recapture energy at the front when designing a regenerative braking system, compared with a rear-wheel layout.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 2. Fundamentals of Conventional Vehicles and Powertrains

qa.evidence_quote: In high-performance vehicles, up to 80% of the braking force may be on the front axle

qa.question: Why is a continuously variable transmission attractive for fuel economy?
qa.answer: A CVT provides an effectively infinite number of gear ratios within its range, so the engine can be held at its most economical speed across a wide span of vehicle speeds. That lets the powertrain track an ideal torque-speed profile that fixed-ratio gearboxes cannot match.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 2. Fundamentals of Conventional Vehicles and Powertrains

qa.evidence_quote: This allows the engine to operate under the most economical conditions over a wide range of vehicle speeds.

qa.question: Why combine a battery with an ultracapacitor in an EV energy storage system?
qa.answer: A battery provides high energy density but cannot easily handle sharp bursts of current. Pairing it with an ultracapacitor lets the capacitor supply or absorb high-power transients during acceleration and braking, reducing battery stress, extending battery life, and improving overall energy capture.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Battery/UC hybrid operation provides an improved solution over the stand-alone battery design in terms of improved power management and control flexibility.

qa.question: What is battery C-rate?
qa.answer: The C-rate is a parameter expressing how intensely a battery is being discharged relative to its capacity. Low C-rates tend to lengthen battery life span, so designers try to avoid instantaneous high-power charge or discharge pulses, which raise stress, heat, and internal resistance.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Battery C-rate is defined as the parameter, which expresses battery discharge intensity

qa.question: What is the main drawback of the passive parallel battery and ultracapacitor topology?
qa.answer: In the passive parallel configuration the battery and ultracapacitor share a bus with no controller between them, so the battery cannot have its current shaped or limited. Whenever the ultracapacitor draws or supplies current, the battery follows the same profile for voltage equalisation, increasing battery stress.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Battery current is, therefore, not effectively controlled

qa.question: Why is the parallel-connected multi-converter topology said to be the most flexible?
qa.answer: Because each storage device has its own bidirectional DC/DC converter, the battery and ultracapacitor voltages are fully decoupled from the DC link and from each other. Power flows and controls are independent, which improves stability, efficiency, and reliability since one source can keep operating if another fails.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: This topology offers the highest flexibility and provides better functionality than that of the cascaded converters topology.

qa.question: How does a flywheel energy storage system store energy?
qa.answer: A flywheel stores energy kinetically as a large rotating disk with a very high moment of inertia, spinning between roughly 20,000 and 50,000 rpm. A motor-generator either speeds up the flywheel to store energy or is driven by it to release energy when needed.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: a flywheel stores energy in a kinetic device rather than in an electrochemical or electrostatic device

qa.question: Why must superconducting magnetic energy storage operate at very low temperatures?
qa.answer: SMES coils only act as superconductors and avoid ohmic losses when chilled to very low temperatures. A cryogenic refrigeration system is therefore required, and its energy use and complexity cut the overall efficiency, which is a serious obstacle to using SMES at vehicle scale.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: needing to be operated at very low temperatures for their coils to act as a superconducting material and reduce ohmic losses

qa.question: Among the simulated battery/UC topologies, which gave the lowest DC link voltage ripple?
qa.answer: The parallel-connected multi-converter topology gave the smallest DC-link voltage ripple, with a maximum of 400.7 V and minimum of 397.6 V (about 0.8 percent). The passive parallel and cascaded converter topologies each saw roughly 2.4 to 2.5 percent ripple over the same drive cycle.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: the DC link voltage sees a maximum of 400.7 V and a minimum of 397.6 V, resulting in a maximum ripple percentage of 0.8%

qa.question: What is a solar powered charging station?
qa.answer: A solar powered charging station, or SPCS, is an EV charging site whose electricity is generated by solar panels. The panels typically also shade the parking area, and most installations are tied to the local electrical grid so power is available even when sunlight is not.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Solar powered charging stations (SPCSs) are one of the important developments related to the electrification of transportation.

qa.question: Why are parking lots a particularly suitable place to install solar panels?
qa.answer: There are far more parking spaces than vehicles in the United States, and many spaces sit empty across stadiums, churches, shopping centres, and workplaces. Covering these unused spaces with panels adds clean generation, provides shade, and could supply a meaningful share of national electricity.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: there are many more parking spaces than vehicles because there are always many empty parking spaces at any given time

qa.question: What is Level 1 EV charging?
qa.answer: Level 1 EV charging uses a standard 120-volt AC household outlet through a cord usually supplied with the vehicle. It adds about 5 miles of range, or roughly 2 kWh, per hour - similar to what a single parking space's solar panels produce.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: Level 1 charging often adds about 5 miles of range or about 2 kWh per hour to the batteries.

qa.question: How much range can DC fast charging add in 20 minutes?
qa.answer: DC fast chargers (Level 3) are designed to deliver rapid charging over 20 to 30 minutes, ending at about three-quarters of full charge. In that window they can add roughly 50 to 70 miles of driving range to a typical electric vehicle.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)

qa.evidence_quote: With fast charging 50–70 miles of range are added in 20 min.

qa.question: What is acid rain and what causes it?
qa.answer: Acid rain forms when nitrogen dioxide released by combustion reacts with atmospheric water to make nitric acid, which then comes down dissolved in rainfall. It damages forests in industrialised countries and corrodes historical marble monuments, and sulfur dioxide from fuels makes a similar contribution.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 1. Environmental Impact and History of Modern Transportation

qa.evidence_quote: It also reacts with atmospheric water to form nitric acid (HNO3), which dilutes in rain. This phenomenon is referred to as "acid rain"

qa.question: Why is carbon monoxide so dangerous to people?
qa.answer: Carbon monoxide attaches to the haemoglobin in blood cells in place of oxygen, starving the body's organs of the oxygen they need. The bond is far stronger than oxygen's, so normal body chemistry cannot break it - victims must be treated in pressurised chambers.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 1. Environmental Impact and History of Modern Transportation

qa.evidence_quote: Once carbon monoxide reaches blood cells, it attaches to the hemoglobin in place of oxygen

qa.question: What share of world oil consumption did transportation account for in 1997?
qa.answer: The transportation sector consumed about 49 percent of the oil used in the world in 1997, making it the largest single user of petroleum. Most projected growth in industrialised-country oil demand also comes from transportation, since few economical alternatives existed at the time of the forecast.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 1. Environmental Impact and History of Modern Transportation
source.section: 1.5 Importance of Different Transportation Development Strategies

qa.evidence_quote: the transportation sector is the primary user of petroleum, consuming 49% of the oil used in the world in 1997

qa.question: Who built the first electric vehicle and when?
qa.answer: The first electric vehicle was built by Frenchman Gustave Trouve in 1881. It was a tricycle powered by a 0.1-horsepower DC motor fed by lead-acid batteries, and together with its driver weighed roughly 160 kilograms - far slower than later gasoline cars would be.

source.book: Hybrid and Electric Vehicles Freebook (compilation of selected chapters)
source.chapter: 1. Environmental Impact and History of Modern Transportation

qa.evidence_quote: The first EV was built by Frenchman Gustave Trouvé in 1881. It was a tricycle powered by a 0.1-hp direct current (DC) motor fed by lead-acid batteries.

qa.question: What weight does the SME EV Body of Knowledge give to Manufacturing Foundations and Awareness?
qa.answer: The SME EV Body of Knowledge assigns Manufacturing Foundations and Awareness a weight of 15 percent of the total competency map. That share reflects how much of a learner's effort should go into foundational shop-floor knowledge before tackling EV-specific topics in detail.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1. Manufacturing Foundations/Awareness

qa.question: How important does SME rate General OSHA Standards and Regulations for EV manufacturing?
qa.answer: SME rates General OSHA Standards and Regulations as a High importance topic. The expected competency level is Remember and Understand, meaning a learner should recall the rules and grasp why they exist rather than apply them in advanced engineering analysis tasks.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.1. General OSHA Standards/Regulations

qa.question: Under SME's OSHA subtopics, what does PPE stand for?
qa.answer: Under SME's OSHA subtopics, PPE stands for Personal Protective Equipment. It refers to gear such as gloves, safety glasses, insulated boots and arc-rated clothing that workers must wear to reduce injury risk on an EV manufacturing shop floor every day.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)

qa.question: What safety subtopic does SME list right after Personal Protective Equipment?
qa.answer: Right after Personal Protective Equipment, SME lists Lockout/Tagout as the next OSHA subtopic. Lockout/Tagout covers procedures for de-energising and labelling equipment so workers cannot be hurt while servicing machines that still have stored electrical or mechanical energy inside them.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) 1.1.4. Lockout/Tagout

qa.question: Does the SME EV Body of Knowledge call out high-voltage safety as a separate topic?
qa.answer: Yes, SME calls out High Voltage Safety as its own subtopic under OSHA Standards, along with linked items for High Voltage Cables and a Safety Switch. This emphasis reflects the unique shock hazards present around traction battery packs and inverters.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.1.8. High Voltage Safety

qa.question: What competency level does SME assign to Measurement?
qa.answer: SME assigns Measurement a Medium importance and an Apply and Analyse competency level. So a trainee should not only recall measurement basics but actually use measuring tools, read results correctly and analyse what those numbers mean for a real build.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.2. Measurement

qa.question: How does SME rate Print Reading for EV manufacturing learners?
qa.answer: SME rates Print Reading at Medium importance and at the Apply and Analyse competency level. Learners are expected to interpret engineering drawings well enough to act on them, not just recognise the symbols and basic line conventions used on a print.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.3. Print Reading

qa.question: Where does Fluid Mechanics sit in SME's importance ranking?
qa.answer: Fluid Mechanics sits at Low importance in SME's ranking, with a Remember and Understand competency level. Learners need to know the basics of fluid behaviour, such as flow and pressure, but they are not expected to do detailed engineering calculations on the topic.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.4. Fluid Mechanics

qa.question: What competency does SME ask of learners for Direct Current Electricity?
qa.answer: For Direct Current Electricity, SME asks learners to reach an Apply and Analyse competency level at Medium importance. They should be able to set up DC circuits, take readings and interpret what voltage, current and resistance values are telling them about a system.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.6. Direct Current Electricity

qa.question: Which three quantities does SME list as sub-items under Ohm's Law?
qa.answer: SME lists three sub-items under Ohm's Law: Voltage, Current and Resistance. These are the three quantities tied together by the law itself, so the curriculum makes sure each is learned before students apply the relationship to real EV circuits and components.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 1.7.1. Voltage 1.7.2. Current

qa.question: Why does SME place Situational Awareness at the Evaluate Problems and Create Solutions level?
qa.answer: Situational Awareness is placed at the Evaluate Problems and Create Solutions level because spotting hazards on a live EV line is not just memory work. A technician has to read the surroundings in real time, judge risks and choose a safe response immediately.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Evaluate Problems & Create Solutions

qa.question: How does SME rate the importance of Industry 4.0 and SMART Manufacturing?
qa.answer: SME rates Introduction to Industry 4.0 and SMART Manufacturing as Low importance, with a Remember and Understand competency. Learners need to be familiar with smart-factory concepts and basic terminology, but deep technical mastery is not expected at this stage of training.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Introduction to Industry 4.0 & SMART Manufacturing

qa.question: How much of the SME EV Body of Knowledge is devoted to the Electric Vehicle Overview section?
qa.answer: The Electric Vehicle Overview section is given 20 percent of the total SME EV Body of Knowledge. That is the second-largest share in the curriculum and signals that learners should spend significant time on basic EV concepts and definitions early on.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 2. Electric Vehicle Overview

qa.question: What two vehicle types does SME compare under 'What are Electric Vehicles'?
qa.answer: Under What are Electric Vehicles, SME explicitly compares the Electric Vehicle, abbreviated EV, against the Internal Combustion Engine vehicle, abbreviated ICE. Learners are expected to grasp the differences in propulsion type, energy source and emissions between the two designs.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Electric Vehicle (EV) vs. Internal Combustion Engine (ICE)

qa.question: Which four electric vehicle types does SME list under Types of Electric Vehicles?
qa.answer: SME lists four electric vehicle types: Battery Electric Vehicle, Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle, Hybrid Electric Vehicle and Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle. Each represents a different way of supplying energy to the wheels and shapes its own design and refuelling profile.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Battery Electric Vehicle (BEV)

qa.question: What share of the SME EV Body of Knowledge is given to Components of Electric Vehicles?
qa.answer: Components of Electric Vehicles is given the largest share, 25 percent, of the SME EV Body of Knowledge. That weight tells trainees that most of their study time should be spent on the physical parts that make an EV actually move.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3. Components of Electric Vehicles

qa.question: Which two battery chemistries does SME mention under Battery Selection?
qa.answer: Under Battery Selection, SME mentions two chemistries: Lithium-ion and Lithium Polymer. Both store energy by moving lithium ions, but they use different electrolyte and packaging styles, and a learner is expected to recognise where each one is typically applied.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Lithium-ion 3.2.2. Lithium Polymer

qa.question: Which three battery cell formats does SME include under Battery Cells?
qa.answer: Under Battery Cells, SME includes three formats: Cylindrical Cells, Prismatic Cells and Pouch Cells. Each format wraps the same internal layers in a different outer shape, affecting how the cells are packed, cooled and protected inside a battery module assembly.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Cylindrical Cells 3.3.2. Prismatic Cells

qa.question: What does the abbreviation BMS stand for in the SME taxonomy?
qa.answer: In the SME taxonomy, the abbreviation BMS stands for Battery Management System. It is the electronics that watch every cell, balance their charge, cut the pack off in faults and let the rest of the vehicle know what the battery is doing.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Battery Management System (BMS)

qa.question: Which three subtopics does SME group under Battery State Determination?
qa.answer: SME groups three subtopics under Battery State Determination: User Interface, Electrical Control and Safety Protection. Together they cover how the BMS tells the driver what is happening, how it controls the pack electrically and how it intervenes when something goes wrong.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Battery State Determination

qa.question: What does SME list as the basic items a BMS should monitor?
qa.answer: Under Battery Monitoring, SME lists Current, Voltage, Temperature and Data Acquisition as the items a BMS should track. These four signals together give the system enough information to judge battery health, performance and safety in real time during driving and charging.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Temperature 3.5.4. Data Acquisition

qa.question: Why does SME flag Thermal Management as High importance under Battery Monitoring?
qa.answer: Thermal Management is flagged as High importance because battery cells lose life or even catch fire if they run too hot. Keeping pack temperatures inside a narrow window is therefore a safety task, not just a comfort or performance tweak for the driver.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3.5.6. Thermal Management

qa.question: What does SME call the section covering the EV's drive system?
qa.answer: SME calls the drive system the EV Traction or Propulsion System. It groups together the EV Motor, the Direct Current Motor and the Alternating Current Motor, since these are the main motor types that turn stored electrical energy into wheel rotation.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: EV Traction/Propulsion System

qa.question: What does the abbreviation SCR mean in SME's EV Speed and Power Controller topic?
qa.answer: In SME's EV Speed and Power Controller topic, the abbreviation SCR means Silicon-Controlled Rectifier. It is a solid-state switch used in power electronics to control how much current flows to the motor, and so how quickly the vehicle accelerates from rest.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Silicon-Controlled Rectifiers (SCR)

qa.question: Which two braking systems does SME list under EV Braking Systems?
qa.answer: SME lists two braking systems under EV Braking Systems: a Regenerative Braking System and a Traditional Braking System. EVs usually combine both, using the motor to slow the car and feed energy back to the battery, while friction brakes handle harder stops.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Regenerative Braking System

qa.question: What does the abbreviation MCB mean under SME's EV Charging Station Components?
qa.answer: Under SME's EV Charging Station Components, MCB means Miniature Circuit Breaker. It is a small automatic switch that opens when too much current flows through it, protecting downstream wiring and chargers from overload or short-circuit damage if a fault occurs.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Miniature Circuit Breakers (MCB)

qa.question: Why does SME mention Surge Protection Devices among charging station components?
qa.answer: SME lists Surge Protection Devices among the charging station components because power-line spikes from lightning or grid faults can destroy the sensitive electronics inside a charger. Surge protectors shunt that excess energy safely away before it ever reaches the charging unit.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Surge Protection Devices

qa.question: Which component does SME list as 3.10.4 in its EV Charging Systems table?
qa.answer: SME lists Onboard Charger as item 3.10.4 in its EV Charging Systems table. The onboard charger sits inside the vehicle and converts the alternating current from a wall socket into the direct current that the traction battery actually accepts during charging.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3.10.4. Onboard Charger

qa.question: What does SME list as item 3.10.2 in EV Charging Systems and Technology?
qa.answer: SME lists DC to DC Converter as item 3.10.2. This converter takes the high-voltage direct current from the traction pack and steps it down to the lower voltages needed to run lights, infotainment and other onboard accessories on the vehicle.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3.10.2. DC/DC Convertor

qa.question: Why does the SME charging table include Thermal System as a charging component?
qa.answer: The charging table includes Thermal System because fast charging dumps large amounts of power into a pack and the resulting heat must be carried away. Without coolant flow the cells would overheat, throttle their charging speed and lose useful service life over time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3.10.5. Thermal System (Cooling)

qa.question: Which three charging station tiers does SME describe at items 3.10.9 through 3.10.11?
qa.answer: Items 3.10.9 to 3.10.11 describe three charging station tiers: a Level 1 Charging Station, a Level 2 Charging Station and a Level 3 Charging Station. The tiers move from slow home outlets up to fast public chargers that refill a pack in minutes.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3.10.11. Level 3 Charging Station

qa.question: What does SME include alongside the wired charger tiers in its charging-systems list?
qa.answer: Alongside the Level 1, 2 and 3 wired chargers, SME includes a Wireless Charging Station in the list. Wireless charging uses a coil in the ground and a matching coil under the car, transferring energy by magnetic induction with no cable plugged in.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 3.10.12. Wireless Charging Station

qa.question: What weight does SME give to EV Manufacturing Processes in its curriculum?
qa.answer: SME gives EV Manufacturing Processes a weight of 10 percent of the curriculum. That share covers the practical shop methods, such as joining, welding, assembly and finishing, that physically turn raw parts into a finished and tested electric vehicle on the line.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 4. EV Manufacturing Processes 10%

qa.question: Which welding method does SME single out in its EV Manufacturing Processes section?
qa.answer: Within EV Manufacturing Processes, SME singles out Laser Welding Technology as its own subtopic at Medium importance. Laser welding is used heavily in EV manufacture for joining battery tabs and other small metal parts where precision and clean heat input really matter.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 4.3. Laser Welding Technology

qa.question: Where does Adhesives appear in SME's manufacturing-processes list?
qa.answer: Adhesives appears as item 4.8 in SME's manufacturing-processes list, at Medium importance. Bonded joints made with structural glues are common in EV bodies, where they replace some welds and rivets to save weight and to seal joints against moisture and dust.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 4.8. Adhesives

qa.question: How much weight does SME place on Robotics in its EV competency map?
qa.answer: SME places 15 percent of its total weight on Robotics in the EV competency map. That share equals Manufacturing Foundations, signalling that hands-on robot skills are now treated as a core element of modern EV production work alongside traditional craft skills.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 5. Robotics 15%

qa.question: What does the SME taxonomy call a Cobot?
qa.answer: The SME taxonomy calls a Cobot a Collaborative Robot. Cobots are designed to share the same workspace with humans, slowing or stopping automatically on contact so operators can hand them parts or guide them without being shut behind a safety fence first.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Collaborative Robots (Cobots)

qa.question: What does SCARA stand for in SME's robot-types list?
qa.answer: In SME's robot-types list, SCARA stands for Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm. It is a four-axis arm that is stiff vertically but able to swing freely in the horizontal plane, making it popular for high-speed pick-and-place assembly tasks on production lines.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Selective Compliance Assembly Robot Arm (SCARA)

qa.question: What name does SME give to a Delta-style robot in its taxonomy?
qa.answer: SME gives the Delta-style robot the formal name Parallel Robot in its taxonomy, listed at item 5.1.10. Parallel robots use several linked arms moving the same end effector, giving very high speed and stiffness over a relatively small working volume.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Parallel Robot (Delta Robot)

qa.question: What does SME describe as a Light Curtain in its robot-safety list?
qa.answer: SME describes a Light Curtain as a presence-sensing safety device in its robot-safety list. It is a wall of invisible infrared beams across an opening, and if anything breaks the beams the nearby robot stops immediately, replacing a physical fence in many cells.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 5.2.8. Light Curtain

qa.question: What does an e-stop do, according to SME's robot-safety list?
qa.answer: According to SME's robot-safety list, an e-stop is an emergency stop button. Pressing one immediately cuts power or motion to a robot or cell, giving operators a fast, single-action way to halt a machine if something dangerous is unfolding on the floor.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 5.2.7. e-stops

qa.question: Which standards bodies does SME list under Robot Safety Guidelines and Standards Awareness?
qa.answer: SME lists OSHA, ANSI/RIA, ISO, UL, IEC and NIOSH under Robot Safety Guidelines and Standards Awareness. Learners are expected to know that several different bodies set complementary rules covering workplace safety, robot-specific design and electrical-product certification of equipment.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: OSHA, ANSI/RIA, ISO, UL, IEC, NIOSH

qa.question: Which four kinds of robot accidents does SME list under Types of Accidents Caused by Robots?
qa.answer: SME lists four kinds of robot accidents: Impact or Collision, Trapping, Crushing and Equipment-related. Together these capture the main ways a human can be hurt by an industrial arm, whether by being struck, pinned, squashed or hit by malfunctioning tooling parts.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge
source.section: 5.5.2 Types of Accidents Caused by Robots

qa.evidence_quote: Impact/Collison 5.5.2.2. Trapping

qa.question: What weight does SME give to Additive Manufacturing in its EV curriculum?
qa.answer: SME gives Additive Manufacturing a small weight of 5 percent in its EV curriculum. The section is mostly Low importance and Remember and Understand, because three-dimensional printed parts are still a niche use in mass EV production rather than a mainstream method.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 6. Additive Manufacturing

qa.question: How much weight does SME give to Lean Manufacturing in its EV curriculum?
qa.answer: SME gives Lean Manufacturing a weight of 2.5 percent in its EV curriculum. Although the share is small, the section covers core efficiency ideas such as 5S principles, the seven forms of waste and continuous improvement that every modern factory expects.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 7. Lean Manufacturing

qa.question: What do the five S's of SME's Lean Manufacturing principle stand for?
qa.answer: The five S's of SME's Lean Manufacturing principle stand for Sort, Set in Order, Sweep, Standardize and Sustain. They form a workplace organisation routine that keeps tools, parts and information arranged so workers can find what they need without wasted motion.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 5S Principles (Sort, Set in Order, Sweep, Standardize, Sustain)

qa.question: What does TQM stand for in SME's Quality section?
qa.answer: In SME's Quality section, TQM stands for Total Quality Management. It is the broader management approach in which every department, not just inspection, is held responsible for delivering products that meet customer expectations the first time, every time, on every build.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: Total Quality Management (TQM)

qa.question: What is Metrology, as used in SME's Quality section?
qa.answer: Metrology, as used in SME's Quality section at item 8.4, is the science of measurement itself. Trainees are expected to understand how measuring tools are calibrated, traced to standards and used so that inspection results are accurate and trustworthy on every part.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 8.4. Metrology

qa.question: Why does SME treat Effective Communication and Working in Teams as Essential Skills?
qa.answer: SME treats Effective Communication and Working in Teams as Essential Skills because EV production lines mix electricians, mechanics, robot programmers and quality staff. Without clear handoffs and shared language, even small misunderstandings can cause expensive defects or serious workplace safety incidents.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge

qa.evidence_quote: 9.1. Effective Communication

qa.question: Which two SME sections together account for almost half of the EV competency weights?
qa.answer: The Components of Electric Vehicles section at 25 percent and the Electric Vehicle Overview section at 20 percent together account for 45 percent of SME's weights, just under half. The combined share signals that EV-specific content is the curriculum's main centre of gravity.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Body of Knowledge
source.chapter: 2. Electric Vehicle Overview / 3. Components of Electric Vehicles

qa.evidence_quote: 3. Components of Electric Vehicles

qa.question: Who authored the NREL Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles slide deck?
qa.answer: The NREL Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles slide deck was authored by Prateek Joshi and Carishma Gokhale-Welch of the National Renewable Energy Laboratory in November 2022. It was prepared as a primer rather than a fully comprehensive reference on every EV topic.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Prateek Joshi and Carishma Gokhale-Welch National Renewable Energy Laboratory November 2022

qa.question: Which university first hosted the course that produced this NREL slide deck?
qa.answer: The course that produced this NREL slide deck was first hosted by the Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology, abbreviated BUET, in October 2022. It was an Energy Fundamentals Course aimed at postgraduates and working professionals from Bangladesh's energy sector.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Energy Fundamentals Course hosted by the Bangladesh University of Engineering and Technology (BUET)

qa.question: Which US agency partnered with NREL to organise the EV fundamentals course?
qa.answer: The United States Agency for International Development, known as USAID, partnered with NREL to help organise the course. The collaboration was meant to share US EV knowledge with engineering students and professionals in countries that are still building out their electric transport markets.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: partnership with the United States Agency for International Development (USAID)

qa.question: What four top-level topics does the NREL EV fundamentals deck cover?
qa.answer: The NREL EV fundamentals deck covers four top-level topics: EV Trends, EV Technology, EV Charging and EV Policies. Each section breaks down further into either global and regional trends, vehicle types and opportunities, charging infrastructure and grid impact, or policy options and case studies.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 1. EV Trends 2. EV Technology 3. EV Charging 4. EV Policies

qa.question: What two scales of EV trends does NREL break out in this deck?
qa.answer: NREL breaks out two scales of EV trends in this deck: global trends and regional trends. The split lets the deck show the world-wide picture of EV adoption first, then zoom in on country-by-country dynamics that vary a lot from one market to another.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: a. Global trends b. Regional trends

qa.question: What time span does NREL's chart of global electric passenger car stock cover?
qa.answer: NREL's chart of global electric passenger car stock covers the period from 2011 to 2021, a decade in which the worldwide fleet of plug-in cars grew from a handful of pilot deployments to millions of units. The figure is sourced from Bloomberg New Energy Finance.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Global electric passenger car stock, 2011-2021

qa.question: Through which year does NREL's Bloomberg projection of EV stock run?
qa.answer: NREL's Bloomberg projection of global electric passenger car stock runs out to 2040. By stretching the timeline this far the chart shows roughly where today's adoption curve is heading even though the actual year-by-year outcome will depend heavily on policy choices.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Global electric passenger car stock projection, 2022-2040

qa.question: Why does NREL say EVs typically cost more upfront than internal-combustion cars?
qa.answer: NREL says EVs typically cost more upfront than internal-combustion cars because of the cost of the battery. The battery pack is the most expensive single component on the vehicle, so until pack prices come down, sticker prices stay above an equivalent petrol car's.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Higher upfront cost of most EVs compared to internal combustion engine (ICE) counterpart is due to cost of battery

qa.question: By how much have EV battery pack prices fallen since 2010, according to NREL?
qa.answer: EV battery pack prices have fallen 89 percent since 2010, according to NREL, despite recent supply-chain disruption. That long-running drop is the single biggest reason that EVs have moved from niche vehicles to mainstream products over little more than a decade in real terms.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Battery pack prices have fallen 89% since 2010

qa.question: When does NREL expect EVs to reach unsubsidised price parity with ICE cars?
qa.answer: NREL expects EVs to reach unsubsidised upfront price parity with internal-combustion cars in most vehicle segments and markets by the late 2020s. Until then, government incentives are what bridges the gap for many buyers comparing sticker prices on the showroom floor.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Unsubsidized upfront price parity expected in most vehicle segments and markets by the late 2020s

qa.question: Why does NREL argue that EVs are already cheaper to own over their lifetime?
qa.answer: NREL argues EVs are already cheaper to own over their lifetime because they have lower fuel and maintenance expenses than the internal-combustion equivalent. Electricity per mile costs less than petrol, and an electric drivetrain has far fewer moving parts that need servicing as it ages.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: lifetime operational cost of owning EVs is typically lower than the ICE counterpart due to reduced fuel and maintenance expenses

qa.question: By how much did public EV charging stations grow between 2015 and 2021?
qa.answer: Public EV charging stations grew by 40 percent between 2015 and 2021, according to NREL. The expansion was uneven across countries but shows that public infrastructure has been catching up with rising EV sales, particularly along major travel corridors and in cities.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 40% increase in publicly available charging stations between 2015 and 2021

qa.question: What was the 2021 global average ratio of EVs to public charging points?
qa.answer: The 2021 global average was 10 EVs per public charging point. That ratio shows how heavily today's public network is shared, and it is one of the headline numbers used to track whether infrastructure is keeping up with vehicle adoption across the world.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 2021 global average: 10 EVs per charging point

qa.question: What does Bloomberg project for EVs per ultra-fast charger by 2040?
qa.answer: Bloomberg projects between 100 and 300 EVs per ultra-fast charger by 2040, NREL reports. That much higher ratio than today reflects the expectation that drivers will charge at home most of the time and visit public ultra-fast chargers only on longer trips.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 100-300 EVs per ultra-fast charger

qa.question: What 2030 EV sales target does India have for passenger cars?
qa.answer: India has set a 2030 EV sales target of 30 percent for passenger cars. That figure sits inside a wider national plan that also covers commercial vehicles and the smaller two- and three-wheelers that account for most road vehicles sold in India.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 30% passenger cars

qa.question: What 2030 EV sales target does India have for commercial vehicles and two- or three-wheelers?
qa.answer: India targets 70 percent of commercial vehicle sales and 80 percent of two- and three-wheeler sales being electric by 2030. The higher share for small vehicles reflects how dominant scooters and rickshaws are in Indian traffic, and how readily they can be electrified.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 70% commercial vehicles, and 80% of twoand three-wheelers

qa.question: What 2030 sales pledge has Toyota made for electrified vehicles?
qa.answer: Toyota has pledged that EVs will make up 40 percent of its annual sales by 2030, according to NREL. The figure includes the company's full range of battery and other electrified vehicles and is one example of the targets major automakers have published.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Toyota: EVs constitute 40% of annual sales by 2030

qa.question: How many EV models is Honda planning by 2030, per NREL's notes?
qa.answer: Honda is planning 30 EV models and production of 2 million units by 2030, per NREL's notes. Combined with similar pledges from Nissan, who aims for 50 percent EV and hybrid sales by 2030, Japan's major automakers have set notable electrification targets in their respective announcements.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Honda: 30 EV models and production of 2 million units by 2030

qa.question: What does NREL list as the third top-level topic in this fundamentals deck?
qa.answer: NREL lists EV Charging as the third top-level topic in this fundamentals deck, after EV Trends and EV Technology and before EV Policies. The charging section is broken down further into charging infrastructure and the impact charging has on the electricity grid.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 3. EV Charging

qa.question: How does NREL define a Hybrid Electric Vehicle in the drive-train slide?
qa.answer: NREL defines a Hybrid Electric Vehicle as a vehicle with an internal-combustion engine and an electric motor whose batteries are charged by the engine itself. There is no external charging port, so the driver tops it up only at a petrol pump.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Hybrid Electric Vehicle

qa.question: What separates a Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle from a Hybrid Electric Vehicle, per NREL?
qa.answer: A Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle keeps both an internal-combustion engine and an electric motor, but its batteries are rechargeable from an external source. That extra charging option lets it drive on electricity alone for short trips before the engine has to step in.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Plug-in Hybrid Electric Vehicle

qa.question: How does NREL describe a Battery Electric Vehicle in the drive-train slide?
qa.answer: NREL describes a Battery Electric Vehicle as a 100 percent electric motor driven vehicle whose batteries are rechargeable from an external source. With no internal-combustion engine on board, all of the energy used to move the vehicle has to come from the battery pack.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Battery Electric Vehicle

qa.question: How is a Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle different in NREL's drive-train slide?
qa.answer: A Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle in NREL's drive-train slide is 100 percent electric motor driven, but its electricity comes from a fuel cell that converts hydrogen and oxygen into power. The car requires a hydrogen distribution infrastructure to refuel, which is still thin in most countries today.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Fuel cell converts hydrogen and oxygen into electricity

qa.question: Why does NREL say vehicle fleets are well suited to electrification?
qa.answer: NREL says vehicle fleets such as taxis, three-wheeler services and delivery vans are well suited to electrification because they have high kilometres travelled, fixed routes and predictable schedules. Those traits squeeze the most savings out of lower running costs and reduce the need for fast charging.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: High vehicle-kilometers-traveled, fixed-route operation, and predictable schedules

qa.question: Which four EV benefits does NREL highlight on its opportunities slide?
qa.answer: NREL highlights reduced maintenance and fuel costs, increased fuel efficiency, reduced greenhouse gas emissions and economic and job opportunities as four EV benefits on its opportunities slide. The list captures both wallet-level savings and broader societal gains that policymakers care about.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Reduced maintenance Increased fuel Reduced greenhouse Economic and job

qa.question: What four extra EV benefits round out NREL's opportunities slide?
qa.answer: Rounding out NREL's opportunities slide are zero tailpipe emissions with improved air quality, falling battery costs, enhanced energy security and performance benefits such as quiet operation. Together these complement the cost and emissions benefits already listed on the same slide.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Zero tailpipe Falling costs for Enhanced energy Performance benefits

qa.question: Which five EV challenges does NREL list on its challenges slide?
qa.answer: NREL lists five EV challenges: increased electricity demand, higher upfront costs for some segments, charging infrastructure buildout, access to critical minerals for batteries and workforce development. Each of these has to be managed for EV adoption to scale smoothly across an economy in practice.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Increased electricity demand Higher upfront costs for some segments Charging infrastructure buildout Access to critical minerals for batteries Workforce development

qa.question: What does the acronym NZE stand for in NREL's projection scenarios?
qa.answer: In NREL's projection scenarios on the challenges slide, NZE stands for the net zero emissions by 2050 scenario. It sits alongside STEPS, the stated policies scenario, and APS, the announced pledges scenario. All three describe different futures for electricity demand and EV uptake.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: NZE: net zero emissions by 2050 scenario STEPS: stated policies scenario APS: announced pledges scenario

qa.question: What four purposes does NREL list for any type of EV charging station?
qa.answer: NREL lists four purposes for any EV charging station: connecting the EV to the grid, providing a dedicated circuit so the wiring is not overloaded, ensuring a safe connection before power flows and preventing damage to the EV battery during the charging cycle.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Connects EV to grid Dedicated circuit prevents overloading Safe connection before power can flow Prevents EV battery damage

qa.question: What share of charging events typically happens at home, per NREL?
qa.answer: Per NREL, internationally 50 to 80 percent of all charging events happen at home. That dominance is why most established EV markets focused on residential charging first, and why a lack of home-charging availability is often called a barrier to wider EV adoption.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 50%-80% of all charging events occurred at residences

qa.question: What does NREL abbreviate as EVSE in the residential charging slide?
qa.answer: In the residential charging slide, NREL abbreviates EVSE as Electric Vehicle Supply Equipment. It is the formal name for the cordset, wallbox or post that delivers electricity to a vehicle. Residential charging can use either Level 1 or Level 2 EVSE depending on outlet type.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Residential charging can use Level 1 or Level 2 EV supply equipment (EVSEs)

qa.question: How fast is Level 2 EVSE charging, per NREL's charging speed slide?
qa.answer: Per NREL's charging-speed slide, a Level 2 EVSE adds about 16 to 32 kilometres per hour of charging. The exact speed is often limited by the vehicle's onboard charger, but a typical rating is 7.2 kilowatts at 240 volts of alternating current.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Level 2 EVSE 16–32 km per hour of charging

qa.question: How fast can a DC fast charger replenish an EV, per NREL?
qa.answer: Per NREL, a DC fast charger can replenish 95 to 128 kilometres of range per hour of charging at a typical rating of 50 kilowatts and 480 to 600 volts direct current. The top end of today's units now reaches up to 350 kilowatts of power.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: DC Fast Charger 95–128 km per hour of charging

qa.question: Why does NREL note that DC fast chargers are incompatible with many two- and three-wheelers?
qa.answer: NREL notes DC fast chargers are incompatible with many two- and three-wheelers because those small vehicles use lower-voltage battery systems that cannot accept the high-power DC output. They are also too small to need that speed, so the equipment cost would not be justified.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Incompatible with many 2and 3-wheelers

qa.question: Why does NREL say battery swapping is more compatible with renewable energy than wired EVSE?
qa.answer: NREL says battery swapping is more compatible with renewable energy than wired EVSE because packs can be charged whenever wind or solar output is high and held until a rider needs one. The vehicle does not have to wait at the station while charging happens.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: More compatible with renewables than EVSE

qa.question: Which three battery-swap network operators does NREL name?
qa.answer: NREL names three large battery-swap network operators: Gogoro in Taiwan, Immotor in China and KYMCO also in Taiwan. Their networks let scooter riders trade a depleted pack for a charged one at a kiosk in less time than it takes to refuel a petrol scooter.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)
