qa.evidence_quote: Largest networks operated by Gogoro (Taiwan), Immotor (China), KYMCO (Taiwan)

qa.question: Which four motorcycle makers does NREL say have formed a swappable battery consortium?
qa.answer: NREL says Honda, KTM, Piaggio and Yamaha have formed a swappable battery consortium to agree common standards. A shared standard means a single pack design can fit different brands of scooter, which makes swap stations more useful and helps the technology scale.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Honda, KTM, Piaggio, and Yamaha have formed a swappable battery consortium

qa.question: Which charging standard does NREL list for North America?
qa.answer: NREL lists SAE J1772 as North America's charging standard. It uses a 5-pin alternating-current port known as Type 1 for slower charging, and pairs with a 7-pin direct-current port called the Combined Charging Standard 1, abbreviated CCS1, for fast charging.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: SAE J1772 – North America

qa.question: What standard family does NREL list for Europe and emerging markets?
qa.answer: NREL lists IEC 61851 and IEC 62196 as Europe and emerging markets' charging standards. They use a 7-pin alternating-current port called Type 2 for slower charging and a 9-pin direct-current port called the Combined Charging Standard 2, or CCS2, for faster charging.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: IEC 61851/62196 Europe and emerging markets

qa.question: Which charging standards does India accept, according to NREL?
qa.answer: According to NREL, India accepts a mix of charging standards: Bharat Standards for low-power charging, CCS, CCS2, CHAdeMO and Tesla. The variety reflects that India imports vehicles from many regions and is still settling on which standards its public network should converge on.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: India has Bharat Standards (low power), CCS, CCS2, CHAdeMO, and Tesla

qa.question: Why does NREL describe parked cars as underutilised assets for the grid?
qa.answer: NREL describes parked cars as underutilised assets because vehicles in the United States sit parked roughly 95 percent of the time. That long idle window means an EV plugged in for hours could deliver grid services, like absorbing solar peaks or supporting renewable integration.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Parked \~95% of the time (in United States)

qa.question: Why does NREL argue that EVs are not just a burden to the grid?
qa.answer: NREL argues EVs are not just a burden to the grid because flexible charging can satisfy a driver's mobility needs while also supporting the grid and the integration of renewable energy. Flexibility is secondary to mobility but is enabled by the right charging infrastructure choices.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: flexible EV charging can satisfy mobility needs while also supporting the grid and integration of renewable energy

qa.question: Which incentive does NREL's effectiveness table say boosts US plug-in EV purchases most?
qa.answer: In NREL's effectiveness table, high-occupancy vehicle lane access is the strongest lever, lifting plug-in EV per-capita purchases by 8.3 percent. Rebates of $1,000 raise sales 4.8 percent, sales tax waivers 3.6 percent and a $1,000 tax credit 2.3 percent by comparison.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)
source.section: EV Purchase Incentives: Effectiveness in the United States

qa.evidence_quote: High-occupancy vehicle lane access

qa.question: How much funding does India's FAME II programme commit to EV purchase incentives?
qa.answer: India's FAME II programme commits $1.4 billion in EV purchase incentives for 1.6 million electric vehicles and hybrids between 2019 and 2024. The programme also includes $133 million for charging infrastructure and direct subsidies for nearly 6,000 electric buses so far.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Purchase incentives of \$1.4B USD for 1.6M EVs and hybrids

qa.question: Why does NREL say India's EV adoption is running behind its FAME II schedule?
qa.answer: NREL says India's EV adoption is running behind the FAME II schedule possibly because the country has no zero-emission vehicle sales requirements or ICE phase-out targets. Industry voices also blame aggressive localisation rules set before the market was large enough to absorb them.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Possibly due to no zero-emission vehicle sales requirements or ICE phase-out targets

qa.question: How many EVs and public chargers does NREL list for Barbados?
qa.answer: NREL lists 430 EVs on the road in Barbados, served by 45 public chargers and 200 private chargers. Those figures give the country the highest number of EVs per capita in the Caribbean despite being a small island state with a modest total vehicle fleet.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 430 EVs on the road, 45 public chargers, 200 private chargers

qa.question: By how much did Barbados cut its EV import taxes?
qa.answer: Barbados cut its EV import taxes from 45 percent down to 10 percent, NREL reports. That steep drop is part of the country's pilot package alongside pilot projects, EV maintenance courses for technicians and an open charging-market policy that admits independent operators.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Reduced import taxes on EVs (from 45% to 10%)

qa.question: How many EVs did New Zealand sell in 2021, per NREL's case study?
qa.answer: Per NREL's case study, New Zealand sold 10,574 EVs in 2021. The country's Emission Reduction Plan also promotes walking, cycling and public transit alongside EVs, signalling that electrification of cars is only one piece of the country's wider transport decarbonisation strategy.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: 10,574 EVs sold in 2021

qa.question: What emissions threshold defines a New Zealand Clean Car Discount Programme rebate?
qa.answer: Under New Zealand's Clean Car Discount Programme, vehicles emitting less than 146 grams of carbon dioxide per kilometre qualify for a rebate, while vehicles above that limit pay a fee. The scheme applies to both new and used vehicles and covers a wide market range.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Rebates for vehicles (new and used) emitting less than 146 grams of CO2 per kilometer

qa.question: Which NREL reference does this deck cite for projecting US end-use electrification costs to 2050?
qa.answer: This deck cites the NREL Electrification Futures Study by Jadun and colleagues, published as report NREL/TP-6A20-70485 in 2017. That study projects end-use electric technology costs and performance through 2050 and is the source for several of the deck's electricity demand and battery cost figures.

source.book: Fundamentals of Electric Vehicles (EVs)

qa.evidence_quote: Electrification Futures Study: End-Use Electric Technology Cost and Performance Projections through 2050

qa.question: Why are lithium-ion batteries currently the only choice for electric vehicle batteries?
qa.answer: Lithium-ion batteries are picked for EVs because they store a lot of energy and power for their size, last a long time, are built in modular packs, and have become relatively cheap. Other chemistries like lead-acid cannot match this combination of strengths in cars.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 1.1.2 Current Lithium-Ion Battery Technologies and Materials

qa.evidence_quote: LIBs are the main choice for consumer electronics, electric-drive vehicles, and grid energy storage due to their high energy and power, longevity, modularity, and relatively low cost.

qa.question: How large is a typical EV battery pack compared to a hybrid car battery?
qa.answer: Hybrid car batteries are small, holding only 0.6 to 1.4 kilowatt-hours of energy, while a full electric vehicle pack holds 40 to 100 kilowatt-hours. That means an EV pack stores roughly 30 to 150 times more energy than the battery in a typical hybrid.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: These battery sizes range from 0.6–1.4 kWh, whereas an electric vehicle (EV) LIB size ranges from 40–100 kWh.

qa.question: Why is cobalt considered the highest supply risk for EV batteries?
qa.answer: Cobalt is the highest supply risk because reserves are limited, mining concentrates in just a few regions, and the practices used to extract it raise serious environmental and human-rights concerns. Prices can also spike unexpectedly, so manufacturers are racing to reduce or remove cobalt.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Cobalt is the highest material supply risk for EVBs due to a variety of factors, including resource availability, mining practices, and environmental impact.

qa.question: How much do annual lithium-ion battery demands forecast to reach by 2030?
qa.answer: BloombergNEF projects that the world will need over 2 terawatt-hours of lithium-ion batteries every year by 2030. To get a sense of scale, that is roughly twenty times more battery capacity than was used in 2020, driven mostly by EV adoption.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Annual demand for LIBs is projected to exceed 2 TWh by 2030 (BloombergNEF 2019).

qa.question: What does end-of-life mean for an EV battery according to the United States Advanced Battery Consortium?
qa.answer: The US Advanced Battery Consortium defines end-of-life as the point where the battery has lost more than 20 percent of either its original capacity or its original power. Until that point, the pack is still considered fit to power a car normally.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: a battery reaches its end of life when it has lost more than 20% of capacity or power

qa.question: How long do EV manufacturers typically warranty their batteries for?
qa.answer: Most EV makers cover the battery under warranty for 8 to 10 years from purchase. During that period, the manufacturer takes responsibility for any needed repairs or replacements, giving the owner some financial assurance against early degradation or sudden failure of the pack.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: EV manufacturers provide battery warranties for 8–10 years (Najman 2021), taking responsibility for any repairs or replacements.

qa.question: Why might an EV battery last 12 to 16 years inside a car?
qa.answer: Carmakers build in a safety margin so the pack performs well even after some degradation. After the 8 to 10 year warranty, experts say batteries often keep working for another 2 to 6 years, giving a real-world life of roughly 12 to 16 years in the vehicle.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: EVBs can perform well for another 2–6 years beyond warranty, giving a battery life of 12–16 years in a vehicle

qa.question: What is a second-life battery?
qa.answer: A second-life battery is an EV battery that is no longer suitable for powering a car but still has enough capacity left to be useful elsewhere. They typically go into stationary energy storage applications such as backing up homes or the electricity grid for another decade.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Second-life batteries are end-of-first-use EVBs that have use beyond that in a car

qa.question: How long can a second-life battery continue to operate after leaving its vehicle?
qa.answer: After being repurposed out of a car, a second-life battery can keep working for another 10 years or more in less demanding roles. Its remaining capacity is usually more than enough to support stationary energy storage uses such as renewable power buffering.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: the second-life battery can continue to work for another 10 years or more

qa.question: Why might lithium iron phosphate batteries be a poor candidate for recycling today?
qa.answer: Lithium iron phosphate, or LFP, packs contain little to no nickel or cobalt in the cathode. Recovered iron has low market value, so the cost of recycling them often exceeds what the recovered materials are worth, leaving recyclers with a net loss.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: LIBs such as lithium iron phosphate batteries that have low or no nickel or cobalt content in the cathodes are usually of negative value when recycled

qa.question: Which battery safety standard covers second-life EV conversion to stationary storage?
qa.answer: Underwriters Laboratories created the UL 1974 standard specifically for second-life conversions, where used EV batteries are repurposed as stationary energy storage. Related standards include UL 1973 for the energy storage system itself and UL 9540a for fire prevention testing on those systems.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 1.2.3 Second-Life Battery Market Evolution and Challenges

qa.evidence_quote: UL 1974 (second-life EV conversion to stationary energy storage), UL 9450 Energy Storage, and UL 9450a (fire prevention) protocols are very expensive

qa.question: What are the three main lithium-ion battery recycling technologies?
qa.answer: The three major recycling approaches are pyrometallurgy, which uses high-temperature smelting; hydrometallurgy, which uses chemical leaching with acids or other fluids; and direct recycling, which keeps the cathode material intact rather than breaking it down to elemental metals.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: there are three major technologies currently in various stages of commercialization: pyrometallurgy, hydrometallurgy, and direct recycling

qa.question: What is pyrometallurgy in the context of battery recycling?
qa.answer: Pyrometallurgy is the high-temperature smelting of batteries in a furnace to pull out metals and intermediate compounds. The inputs can be whole batteries or shredded black mass, and the outputs are metal alloys and a glassy waste byproduct called slag.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Pyrometallurgy is the process of high-temperature thermal treatment of batteries in a furnace to extract metals and intermediate compounds

qa.question: Why is lithium often lost in standard pyrometallurgical recycling?
qa.answer: During smelting, lighter impurities float to the top of the melted metal and form a layer called slag or dross. Lithium compounds, being light, end up trapped in this slag. The slag is poured off, so the lithium is usually discarded unless extra hydrometallurgical steps are added.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: the dross also captures the much lighter (in terms of density) lithium compounds. The dross is then poured off

qa.question: Why are hydrometallurgy plants considered easier to build than pyrometallurgy plants?
qa.answer: Hydrometallurgy uses chemical leaching at low temperatures rather than energy-hungry smelting, so the plants are smaller, use much less energy, and need less capital investment. Permitting tends to be simpler too, which speeds up construction in the United States and Canada.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Hydrometallurgical plants use much less energy than pyrometa llurgy plants. In addition, the plant size can be much smaller

qa.question: What is direct recycling of lithium-ion batteries?
qa.answer: Direct recycling recovers cathode material while keeping its chemical structure intact, instead of breaking it down into elemental metals and rebuilding the cathode from scratch. Skipping these steps makes it potentially the cheapest and lowest-carbon recycling route once mature.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Direct recycling involves recovery of cathode while maintaining its molecular structure, rather than breaking it down into constituent metals

qa.question: What share of total lithium-ion battery recycling cost comes from transportation?
qa.answer: Roughly half of the cost of recycling any kind of lithium-ion battery is spent on shipping. Batteries are bulky, heavy, and often classified as hazardous material, which forces special packing, low truckload counts, and long trips to scarce recycling facilities.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: About 50% or more of the cost of recycling any type of LIB is transportation (Dai et al. 2019)

qa.question: How much cheaper is rail than truck for long-distance battery transport, and what emissions benefit does it bring?
qa.answer: In 2021, transcontinental rail shipping in the United States ran about 30 percent cheaper than equivalent truck routes. On a Los Angeles to Syracuse trip, rail also avoided around 4,410 pounds of greenhouse gas emissions, or about 1.6 pounds per mile.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: rail would prevent generation of about 4,410 pounds of greenhouse gas emissions compared to truck transit over the 2,768-mile trip, or 1.6 pounds of carbon dioxide per mile

qa.question: What is thermal runaway in a lithium-ion battery?
qa.answer: Thermal runaway is a chain reaction inside a cell where heat triggers a chemical reaction that creates more heat, pushing temperatures higher in milliseconds. The cell can reach around 400 degrees Celsius and release its stored energy suddenly, often starting a fierce fire.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Thermal runaway is a chain reaction within a battery cell that can be very difficult to stop once it has started.

qa.question: What temperature can a battery cell reach during thermal runaway?
qa.answer: A cell undergoing thermal runaway can hit temperatures of about 752 degrees Fahrenheit, which equals 400 degrees Celsius. Those temperatures cause the cell to release gases and can ignite a fire so hot it is nearly impossible to put out using normal methods.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: This chain reaction creates extremely high temperatures (around 752°F, or 400°C).

qa.question: What fraction of total battery mass do the cells themselves account for, and why does that matter for recycling?
qa.answer: Cells make up 50 to 60 percent of the total mass of an EV battery pack. Because they hold most of the valuable cathode metals, recyclers focus on getting cells out cleanly. The rest of the pack mass is modules, casings, wiring, and electronics.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Modules Different electrical designs Cells (50-60% of total battery mass) Different mechanical designs High voltage bus bars

qa.question: How much of each metal can EU recyclers recover from a lithium-ion battery?
qa.answer: European Union figures suggest that hydrometallurgy or pyrometallurgy can recover up to 90 percent of cobalt, nickel, manganese, and aluminum from a lithium-ion battery, plus up to 35 percent of the lithium. Graphite recovery is also possible depending on the process.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Lithium (up to 35%) Additional challenges with cylindrical cells(unwinding spira sheets of cathode, node and separator) Cobalt (up to 90%)

qa.question: Why do composite plastics make EV battery packs harder to recycle?
qa.answer: Polycarbonate blends used in packs are lightweight, stable, and often flame-retardant, but those very properties make them hard to break down back into original feedstock. They can hinder pack repair, cell reuse, and full recycling of plastics, though metals can still be recovered.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: these thermoplastics can help reduce battery weight and provide some thermal protection, unless carefully selected, these materials are often difficult or not practical to recycle to original materials

qa.question: What is a battery-as-a-service business model?
qa.answer: Under battery-as-a-service, you buy the car but not the battery. Instead, you pay the manufacturer or a battery company a monthly fee for the use of the pack. This lowers the upfront price of the EV and lets the owner handle replacement and end-of-life issues.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: the consumer can purchase battery services from the vehicle OEM or battery asset company monthly rather than purchasing the battery with the car

qa.question: How much revenue can battery leasing add per vehicle, according to McKinsey?
qa.answer: The McKinsey Institute estimates that a successful battery leasing program could add more than 1,000 US dollars per vehicle in revenue over an assumed 5-year lease term. The model also gives the manufacturer ownership for second-life resale into stationary storage.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: OEMs could add more than \$1,000 in revenue per vehicle through a successful battery leasing program during an assumed lease term of 5 years

qa.question: What is an environmental handling fee in EV battery management?
qa.answer: An environmental handling fee is a one-time recycling charge added to the price of a new EV. A third party holds the money and uses it later to pay for safely recycling the battery once it leaves the market, removing that worry from the manufacturer.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: This model puts the responsibility for recycling the battery on the user of the vehicle by charging them a fee at time of purchase

qa.question: What does a battery management system, or BMS, actually do?
qa.answer: A BMS is the brain of a rechargeable battery. It watches and controls how the cells charge and discharge, keeping the pack safe and healthy. In an EV, it shares vital data such as temperature, current, voltage, and state of charge with the car's diagnostics system.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 2.2.2 Accessing Battery SOH and SOC When the BMS Is Intact

qa.evidence_quote: It controls and monitors the charge and discharge of rechargeable batteries in EVs, along with cellphones, laptops, and myriad consumer devices.

qa.question: What is coulomb counting and why is it slow for sorting used batteries?
qa.answer: Coulomb counting measures how much electric charge flows into and out of a cell during full charge-discharge cycles to find its remaining capacity. It is very accurate but needs one or two complete cycles, expensive lab gear, and trained operators, which adds time and cost.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 2.2.4 Battery State of Health Capture Outside the Vehicle

qa.evidence_quote: coulomb counting, which essentially completes one to two complete charge/discharge cycles and measures the electrons in and electrons out

qa.question: How does ultrasonic testing speed up grading of used EV batteries?
qa.answer: Ultrasonic testing uses sound waves to assess a cell's state of health in just a few seconds, matching the accuracy of lab-grade cycling equipment. Non-technical operators can run it, which dramatically lowers the cost of preparing used batteries for second-life applications.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 2.2.4 Battery State of Health Capture Outside the Vehicle

qa.evidence_quote: a rapid battery testing technology has been developed using ultrasonic techniques that can perform SOH evaluation with a similar result to lab-grade cycling equipment within a few seconds

qa.question: What top three pieces of information did automakers and suppliers most want about used EV batteries?
qa.answer: The Suppliers Partnership survey found the most valuable information to be the battery's chemistry, the safety and disassembly instructions plus tools required, and the state of health. Knowing these three lets handlers move and process used packs safely and efficiently.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: The top data points identified by responders in the SP survey were: 1. Chemistry 2. Key disassembly and safety instructions and tools required 3. State of health.

qa.question: What is the Battery Passport concept promoted by the Global Battery Alliance?
qa.answer: The Battery Passport is a digital record system that securely shares information about each EV battery. It traces where minerals were sourced, tracks the battery through its life, and documents recycling and carbon footprint, giving every stakeholder verifiable data.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: the Battery Passport, which would provide for securely sharing information and data to identify, validate and track EVB responsible sourcing of minerals

qa.question: Why is private blockchain more practical than public blockchain for battery data sharing?
qa.answer: Public blockchains like Bitcoin require enormous computing power for mining. Private enterprise blockchains such as Hyperledger Fabric Raft do not, using around 10 billion times less energy per transaction. That makes them realistic for supply-chain traceability of batteries.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 2.3.2 Data Sharing Throughout Battery Life Cycles

qa.evidence_quote: Hyperledger Fabric Raft, a type of blockchain framework supporting private solutions, uses 10 billion times less energy per transaction than Bitcoin

qa.question: How much did Chinese government invest in lithium battery industry buildout?
qa.answer: Chinese authorities spent somewhere between 60 and 100 billion US dollars over the past decade growing the domestic lithium-ion industry. The money subsidised cheap electric cars priced around 4,500 US dollars, mining infrastructure, and refining capacity, making China the global leader.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: the Chinese government has spent somewhere between \$60 billion and \$100 billion increasing the domestic market for lithium batteries

qa.question: What does the Inflation Reduction Act offer buyers of qualifying EVs?
qa.answer: Under the 2022 US Inflation Reduction Act, an EV can earn 3,750 US dollars for meeting battery critical-minerals sourcing rules and another 3,750 US dollars for battery component assembly, up to 7,500 US dollars total. The previous 200,000-vehicle cap per automaker was removed.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: may be eligible for a tax credit of \$3,750 for critical minerals, \$3,750 for battery components assembly, or a total of up to \$7,500

qa.question: What is California's target for zero-emission new car sales by 2035?
qa.answer: California's Executive Order N-79-20 requires that 100 percent of in-state sales of new passenger cars and light-duty trucks must be zero-emission by 2035. The state also aims for fully zero-emission medium- and heavy-duty fleets by 2045, with drayage trucks shifting earlier.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: 100% of in-state sales of new passenger cars and light-duty trucks will be zero-emission by 2035

qa.question: What does China's GB/T 3404-2017 standard require of EV makers?
qa.answer: Under GB/T 3404-2017, Chinese EV manufacturers are legally responsible for both recycling end-of-life batteries and judging whether they are suitable for second-life use. The standard also defines codes for tracing packs, modules, and cells through their entire life.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: electric vehicle OEMs are legally responsible for both battery recycling and assessment of second-life potential

qa.question: What does the German Battery Act require manufacturers to do when an EV battery is replaced?
qa.answer: Under the German Battery Act, which took effect on 1 January 2021, the carmaker must take back drive batteries at no charge whenever a pack falls below its guaranteed minimum capacity. The law also allows that battery to be reused in a second-life application instead of disposed.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: manufacturers of electric cars take batteries back from their vehicles at no charge or appoint a disposal partner

qa.question: What is California's Lithium-Ion Car Battery Recycling Advisory Group trying to achieve?
qa.answer: Formed in 2019 under Assembly Bill 2832, this group advises California's legislature with the aim of reusing or recycling as close to 100 percent as possible of every lithium-ion vehicle battery sold in the state, safely and at reasonable cost.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: the goal of ensuring that as close to 100% as possible of lithium-ion vehicle batteries in the state are reused or recycled at end of life

qa.question: Why did Massachusetts voters approve the 2020 Right to Repair law for vehicles?
qa.answer: Massachusetts voters backed expanded Right to Repair by 75 percent to 25 percent in November 2020. The law forces carmakers to share telematics data with vehicle owners and independent garages so they can diagnose and repair modern, electronics-heavy cars from 2022 onward.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: law by a margin of 75% to open car data to vehicle owners and garages

qa.question: What is RAIN RFID and how is it relevant to battery tracking?
qa.answer: RAIN RFID is an ultra-high-frequency radio-frequency identification tag standard that lets readers scan items wirelessly without batteries inside the tag. It is already widely used in the automotive industry for tyre tracking and is being explored for tagging EV battery packs.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle
source.section: 2.3.1 IoT Enabling Batteries and Critical Parts

qa.evidence_quote: a radio frequency identification (RFID) or ultrahigh frequency RFID (RAIN) tag using various standards (either battery-assisted or passive)

qa.question: What is black mass in battery recycling?
qa.answer: Black mass is the dark, powdery material left after a lithium-ion cell is shredded, sieved, and separated from its casing and components. It contains the valuable cathode and anode materials, and recyclers refine it further using chemical or thermal methods to recover metals.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: A mixture of grounded constituents of a lithium-ion battery cell after shredding, sieving, and separation.

qa.question: What does the term digital twin mean for an EV battery?
qa.answer: A digital twin is a virtual representation of a physical battery and its components. It mirrors the real pack's identity, configuration, and condition in software, letting stakeholders query the asset's history and status without having to open or test the hardware itself.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: In terms of electric vehicle batteries, a digital representation of the and components. battery

qa.question: Why was nickel-metal-hydride battery recycling left out of the report's main scope?
qa.answer: Nickel-metal-hydride packs are tiny next to EV batteries and are already being refurbished and resold for hybrid use. The authors focus on lithium-ion batteries because, with the EV boom, the volume of end-of-life lithium-ion packs will dwarf the nickel-metal-hydride stream.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: with large EV market penetration, the amount of end-of-life LIB would be much larger than those of NiMH batteries

qa.question: Which two lithium-ion cathode chemistries today generate positive recycling value?
qa.answer: Of five common cathode types, only two generally pay recyclers more than they cost to process: lithium cobalt oxide, known as LCO, and the nickel-rich mixes lithium nickel manganese cobalt oxide, or NMC, and lithium nickel cobalt aluminum oxide, or NCA, which earn positive value.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Positive Value PricingCathode AcronymCathode Name and General Formulation

qa.question: Why does the design of EV battery packs make them hard to disassemble for recycling?
qa.answer: Each carmaker builds packs differently in electrical layout, mechanical layout, and cell shape, so recyclers face non-uniform designs every time. Add sealed adhesives, rusted bolts, lack of labelling, and unknown chemistries, and stripping a pack safely becomes slow and costly.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: non-uniformity in the design of battery packs isa challnge while disassembling battery packs for recycling

qa.question: What kind of bath chemistry is being developed to free cells from glued battery packs?
qa.answer: Researchers have developed a bath of swelling agents that soaks into glued cells. The chemistry makes urethane, acrylic, silicone, and epoxy adhesives expand until the bond breaks. Cells then pop free without manual prying, and the recycling solution can be reused many times.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: These innovative materials swell the engineered adhesives (urethanes, acrylics, silicone, and epoxies) until the internal stresses within the base polymer exceed its bonding force

qa.question: How much new battery cathode material will global manufacturers need by 2030?
qa.answer: In 2020, lithium-ion batteries consumed about 400,000 tons of cathode material worldwide. By 2030 that figure is projected to climb to roughly 1.2 million tons each year, a tripling that reflects the explosion of EV demand and grid storage needs.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: In 2020, 400,000 tons of cathode materials were used in LIBs, a number that is projected to rise to about 1.2 million tons by 2030

qa.question: What does the European Commission's proposed Batteries Regulation include for recycled content?
qa.answer: The proposed EU Batteries Regulation would require manufacturers to declare how much recycled material their new batteries contain starting in 2025. Then, mandatory minimum percentages of recycled content for cells will phase in, rising in 2030 and increasing again in 2035.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: requirements to declare levels of recycled content in batteries by 2025 and increase the levels required in 2030 and again in 2035

qa.question: What did US auto recyclers value most highly when handling end-of-life EV batteries?
qa.answer: An Institute of Scrap Recycling Industries member survey found that 84 percent of auto recyclers rated safe-handling and removal information as extremely valuable. Market data ranked second and a battery's condition or state of charge ranked third, ahead of vehicle make and model.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: Sample Survey Results From Institute of Scrap Recycling Industries Members on the Value of Information About EVB Attributes

qa.question: Who founded the EverBatt model used to study battery recycling cost and impact?
qa.answer: EverBatt is a closed-loop battery recycling cost and environmental impacts model developed at Argonne National Laboratory and published as report ANL-19/16 by Dai and colleagues in 2019. Researchers use it to compare the costs and emissions of different recycling pathways.

source.book: EV Li-Ion Battery Lifecycle

qa.evidence_quote: EverBatt: A Closed-loop Battery Recycling Cost and Environmental Impacts Model. Lemont, IL: Argonne National Laboratory. ANL -19/16.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What does the eCHIP acronym stand for in this NREL project?
qa.answer: eCHIP stands for the High-Power Electric Vehicle Charging Hub Integration Platform. It is a US Department of Energy funded project that builds an experimental testbed for direct-current, distribution-based fast charging hubs, run jointly by NREL, Argonne, and Oak Ridge labs.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the High-Power Electric Vehicle Charging Hub Integration Platform (eCHIP) project aims to create an experimental platform for integration and control approaches

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Which three US national laboratories collaborate on the eCHIP project?
qa.answer: The eCHIP project is run jointly by three national laboratories. The National Renewable Energy Laboratory, known as NREL, leads the integration testbed, Argonne National Laboratory handles the communication module, and Oak Ridge National Laboratory builds the high-power DC-DC charger module.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The eCHIP project is a multiyear effort conducted by Argonne National Laboratory (Argonne), National Renewable Energy Laboratory (NREL), and Oak Ridge National Laboratory (ORNL)

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the DC bus voltage used in the eCHIP testbed?
qa.answer: The eCHIP testbed operates on a 950-volt direct-current bus voltage. The bus links the grid-tied inverter to the various DC chargers, energy storage emulator, photovoltaic emulator, and building load emulator that together make up the integrated charging hub.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The important hardware ratings used within the scope of this report include 950-V DC bus voltage

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How powerful is the central inverter used in the eCHIP charging hub?
qa.answer: The grid-tied inverter inside the eCHIP testbed is rated for 660 kilowatts of power. It converts the alternating current from the utility grid into the direct current used by the chargers, storage, and emulated solar generation on the hub.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: a 660-kW grid-tied inverter, a 150-kW COTS charger, a 175-kW in-house developed DC-DC charger

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why is a DC-coupled charging hub more efficient than an AC-coupled one when batteries and solar are added?
qa.answer: Batteries, solar panels, and EV chargers all run natively on direct current. A DC hub links them with fewer conversion stages, since each device needs only one isolated DC-DC step rather than the multiple AC-DC and DC-AC stages an AC hub requires.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the EV chargers, energy storage, and PV sys­ tems can all connect to the bus with a single, isolated DC-DC conversion stage

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What voltage range do modern EV battery packs span, and why is that a charger design challenge?
qa.answer: Early EVs had packs around 300 to 400 volts, but newer cars use 800-volt systems. So fast chargers must handle a wide voltage range with multiple conversion stages, filtering, and isolation to safely deliver power across all vehicle types.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: While early EVs had generally low battery pack voltage ranging from 300 V to 400 V, some recent EVs have nominal battery pack voltage as high as 800 V

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why are silicon carbide and gallium nitride devices important for DC charging hubs?
qa.answer: Silicon carbide and gallium nitride are wide-bandgap semiconductor materials that switch on and off very fast with low energy losses. They let designers shrink the heavy 60-hertz isolation transformer to a small, high-frequency unit, making compact and efficient DC-DC chargers feasible.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the recent rise of widebandgap semiconductor technologies such as sili­ con carbide (SiC) or gallium nitride (GaN) devices has enabled the effcient integration of high-frequency

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why can a DC hub's central inverter be sized smaller than an AC hub's inverters?
qa.answer: A DC hub uses one central inverter that handles the average charging demand of the whole site, since storage and chargers share the bus. An AC hub needs an inverter inside every charger sized for that charger's peak, so total inverter capacity is larger and more costly.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The central inverter and grid-connection transformer can be sized to meet the average charging demand, rather than the peak

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why is breaking DC current harder than breaking AC current?
qa.answer: Alternating current naturally crosses through zero amps roughly a hundred times per second, which extinguishes the arc inside a switch. Direct current never reaches zero on its own, so an arc keeps burning unless special DC breakers or solid-state circuit breakers force it out.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: It is more challenging to safely interrupt DC current than AC, since DC current has no zero-crossings.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why are power electronics considered a reliability concern in a DC hub?
qa.answer: Unlike a passive transformer, the central inverter is full of fast-switching power electronics and complex control loops. That makes its statistical reliability lower than a transformer, and because the hub uses just one, a failure can take down the whole site.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The power electronics introduce signifcant reliability concern, since they are a single point of failure for the hub

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What are the two main DC bus topologies for a DC charging hub?
qa.answer: A DC charging hub can use a unipolar bus, with just a positive and negative wire, or a bipolar bus, which adds a neutral wire so loads can connect across half the voltage or the full voltage. Unipolar is still the more common design today.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design
source.section: 2.1.2 Different Types of DC Hub Topologies

qa.evidence_quote: a DC hub can have a unipolar or bipolar DC bus. While unipolar remains the most common DC hub architecture

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why does a bipolar DC bus give better redundancy than a unipolar bus?
qa.answer: A bipolar bus has two independent poles either side of a neutral wire. If one pole fails, the other can keep carrying power at half rating. With unipolar there is just one positive-negative pair, so a single fault disrupts the entire hub instead.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design
source.section: 2.1.2 Different Types of DC Hub Topologies

qa.evidence_quote: Improved redundant operation: If one pole is faulted, the other pole can remain operational.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What voltage are heavy-duty EVs expected to reach in the future?
qa.answer: Heavy-duty EVs are expected to use battery packs as high as 1,500 volts. Inverter technology at that level is already mature in the solar industry, but high-power isolated DC-DC converters at 1,500 volts are still a new, developing technology.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: For HD EVs, the voltage is ex­ pected to reach 1,500 V.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What three isolated DC-DC converter topologies are commonly used in fast chargers?
qa.answer: The three common isolated DC-DC topologies are the LLC, the dual-active bridge or DAB, and the CLLC. All three connect two active bridges through a medium-frequency transformer, and each manages voltage, current, and isolation in slightly different ways.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: There are predominantly three types of topologies to implement isolated DC-DC chargers

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why did the eCHIP team pick the dual-active bridge topology for their charger?
qa.answer: The DAB has strong, flexible control, smaller filter capacitors than rivals, and is naturally bidirectional, which is essential for vehicle-to-grid. Its main weakness, transformer DC bias from imbalance, can be solved through clever control software rather than hardware additions.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the concerns with the DAB converter can be addressed through controls, so it was chosen for the charger.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why is detecting electrical faults harder on DC systems than on AC?
qa.answer: AC systems give protection devices frequency and phase angle information to work with. DC has neither, and power electronics often limit the fault current to less than twice rated. With smaller signals to detect, fault location becomes much trickier on a DC bus.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the ab­ sence of frequency and phasor information in DC systems makes it diffcult for fault detection and localization.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What three functions must any DC hub protection system perform?
qa.answer: A DC charging hub's protection system must do three things to operate safely. It needs to detect when a fault has happened, then isolate the faulted section from the rest of the network, then reconfigure the system so the unaffected parts can keep running.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the protection system needs to perform three functions: i) fault detection, ii) fault isolation, and iii) fault reconfguration

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What are the three main fault types in a DC charging hub?
qa.answer: The three main fault types are pole-to-pole faults, where the positive and negative wires touch directly, pole-to-ground faults, where a wire touches the grounded chassis, and AC grid faults arriving from upstream. Pole-to-ground is the most common in practice.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design
source.section: 2.3.1 Fault Types in a DC Charging Hub

qa.evidence_quote: The major system-level possible fault types in a DC charging hub (based on unipolar confguration) are pole-pole, pole-ground, and AC grid faults

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why are pole-to-pole faults considered the most severe in a DC hub?
qa.answer: A pole-to-pole fault is essentially a direct short across the DC bus with very low resistance. That causes huge fault currents driven both by capacitor discharge and by current flowing through the converter diodes, making it the most damaging fault in the hub.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design
source.section: 2.3.1 Fault Types in a DC Charging Hub

qa.evidence_quote: These types of faults are the most severe faults for a DC hub, as the fault current is very high due to low impedance.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Which industries already have mature DC distribution standards that EV charging can borrow from?
qa.answer: Standards already exist for DC distribution in data centres, residential and commercial buildings, solar photovoltaic systems, offshore wind collection, railway traction networks, and marine and shipboard microgrids. These give EV charging hub designers concepts to adapt rather than write from scratch.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design
source.section: 2.4 DC Power Distribution Standards in Other Industries

qa.evidence_quote: data centers, buildings, PV applica­ tions, offshore DC wind power collection, DC power distribution in rail, and on-board marine DC microgrid

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the Open Charge Point Protocol used for?
qa.answer: The Open Charge Point Protocol, or OCPP, is the de-facto open communication standard for EV charging stations. It lets a charge point talk to a central management server to start and stop sessions, share status, and update firmware, no matter which manufacturer built the hardware.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The OCPP protocol is an open standard that allows monitoring, confguration, and management of EV charging sta­ tions.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How does the MQTT protocol move data between devices?
qa.answer: MQTT uses a publish-and-subscribe model. Each device sends, or publishes, messages to named topics on a central broker, while other devices listen by subscribing to the topics they care about. The broker then forwards each message to the right subscribers.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The protocol operates on a publish/subscribe model where clients publish messages to topics and other clients subscribe to those topics to receive the messages.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is OpenADR and why does it matter for charging hubs?
qa.answer: Open Automated Demand Response, or OpenADR, is a vendor-neutral signalling protocol. Grid operators use it to tell large customers, including EV charging hubs, when to ease back on power use during peak demand. It also supports pricing signals that the hub can react to.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: OpenADR is an open, standardized communication protocol for demand response (DR) management

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What power ratings do the two candidate inverters in the eCHIP testbed have?
qa.answer: Two commercial inverters were chosen. The Anderson AC2660P is rated at 660 kilowatts and is used for initial control testing. The EPC Power CAB1000 reaches 1,043 kilowatts and will support advanced uses like vehicle-to-everything, grid islanding, and reactive-power support later in the project.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: Table 2. Inverters Used in the DC Hub

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the DC-REDB at NREL's Energy Systems Integration Facility?
qa.answer: The DC Research Electrical Distribution Bus, or DC-REDB, is a three-wire DC backbone with positive, negative, and ground conductors. It runs through several labs inside the Energy Systems Integration Facility, connecting different DC resources together, and is rated for 1,000 volts maximum.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: wire (positive, negative, ground) system with three-pole switches and rated for maximum voltage of 1,000 V DC.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How much power can the COTS Tritium PKM150 charger deliver, and to how many EVs?
qa.answer: The Tritium PKM150 is a commercial DC-DC charger that can simultaneously feed up to two EVs at a combined output of 150 kilowatts. One outlet is a CCS plug rated to 350 amps at 920 volts, the other is a CHAdeMO plug rated to 125 amps at 500 volts.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: capable of simultaneously charging up to two EVs with an aggregate charging power of 150 kW

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How does the eCHIP testbed emulate a solar photovoltaic array?
qa.answer: A Magna-Power MTA1000-250 DC power supply rated at 250 kilowatts is used to mimic a real solar array. The supply runs in current-control mode, and the energy management system sends it new current set-points every second using a one-second-resolution real PV power profile.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: A Magna-Power MTA1000-250 MT Series VI DC power supply is used to emulate PV generation.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How much building load can the load bank used in the eCHIP testbed dissipate?
qa.answer: The Simplex Mars resistive load bank can absorb up to 505.5 kilowatts at 1,000 volts DC and 505.5 amps DC. It burns the power as heat in switchable resistive elements, and the resolution of control is limited to about 0.5 kilowatts per step.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The load bank is rated at 505.5 kW, 1,000 V DC, and 505.5 A DC.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What charging powers do the two test EVs in eCHIP support?
qa.answer: The Hyundai Ioniq 5 has an 800-volt pack and can pull up to 235 kilowatts when fast charging. The Nissan Leaf uses a 348-volt pack and tops out at 40 kilowatts. The pair gives the testbed both an 800-volt-class and a lower-voltage vehicle to exercise.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The Ioniq 5 has a nominal pack voltage of 800 V with a maximum charging power capability of 235 kW.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the UPER charger developed by Oak Ridge National Laboratory?
qa.answer: UPER stands for Universal Power Electronics Regulator. It is a custom dual-active-bridge DC-DC charger built at Oak Ridge that connects the DC bus to the vehicle. The first prototype is rated at 175 kilowatts and 1,000 volts and is designed to be scaled up to half a megawatt.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The isolated DC-DC DAB converter is referred to here as a Universal Power Electronics Regulator (UPER), interfacing the DC distribu tion bus and the vehicle.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What efficiency target and switching frequency does the UPER charger aim for?
qa.answer: The UPER 1000-volt-class charger targets an efficiency above 99 percent across its bidirectional operation. It runs at a switching frequency of 20 kilohertz, picked deliberately to keep the acoustic noise out of the audible range and improve user comfort.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The switching frequency has been chosen to be 20 kHz to reduce the acoustic noise.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why does the UPER charger use a transformer tap to serve both 400-volt and 800-volt cars?
qa.answer: A dual-active-bridge converter struggles to keep zero-voltage switching across very wide output ranges. By adding a tap on the secondary side of the medium-frequency transformer, the UPER swaps to the right turns ratio for either a 400-volt or 800-volt pack, preserving efficiency.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The tap is used to provide correct voltage to either a 400-V class vehicle or an 800-V class vehicle.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the SpEC module developed by Argonne?
qa.answer: The Smartgrid EV Communication, or SpEC, module is a small communication controller built by Argonne. It acts as the supply-equipment communications controller for a DC fast charger, translating ISO 15118 and DIN 70121 messages between the vehicle and the charger's power electronics.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The Smartgrid EV Communication (SpEC) module developed by Argonne is a smart EV communication controller that enables DC fast charging communication between an EV and the charger.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How does the SpEC module physically talk to the EV during a charging session?
qa.answer: The SpEC module sits at the charger end and exchanges messages with the car over power line communication, or PLC. The PLC signals travel down the control pilot wire inside the standard CCS connector, so no extra cable is needed to handle ISO 15118 messages.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: the communication be­ tween them is via power line communication (PLC) over the CCS connector’s control pilot line.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the role of a site energy management system, or SEMS?
qa.answer: A SEMS is the brain of a charging hub. It monitors, controls, and optimises how energy flows between chargers, batteries, solar, and the grid. The goal is to cut energy consumption, lower costs, and coordinate the interactions of all the components on the site.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: SEMS involves the implementation of strategies aimed at reducing energy consumption, increasing effciency, minimizing costs, and defning interactions between hub components.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What are the three common objectives of a site energy management strategy?
qa.answer: The three common SEMS goals are reducing the time it takes to charge an EV, minimising the cost of charging for both driver and operator, and optimising load and energy sharing between the hub's chargers, energy storage, generation, and other loads.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: Some of the common objectives of an SEMS strategy, along with their implementation challenges and requirements, are detailed below.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: Why does adding battery storage and solar generation improve a SEMS's resilience?
qa.answer: Renewables and batteries give the hub a decentralized power source, so if the utility grid drops out the hub can keep critical loads alive on its own. It also reduces dependence on a single supply path, lowering the chance of total outage during disturbances.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: Incorporating renewables and ESS into an SEMS can provide a decentralized source that can continue to provide power during grid outages.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What are the trade-offs of a centralized SEMS controller?
qa.answer: A central SEMS controller can optimise the whole site because it sees every measurement in real time. But that one controller becomes a single point of failure, may become a performance bottleneck under load, and is harder to scale up as new chargers are added.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: this architecture can also introduce single point of failure, as the entire system may be disrupted if the central controller fails or becomes overwhelmed

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is droop control in a decentralized DC charging hub?
qa.answer: Droop control is a simple rule that lets each device decide its own power level from the DC bus voltage alone. The device follows a piecewise-linear curve, so no central controller or fast communication is needed. It is the classic decentralized scheme for shared DC buses.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: The traditional droop controller is good example of a decentralized scheme where the power-set points are piece­ wise linear functions of the bus voltage.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: What is the distributed SEMS architecture meant to combine?
qa.answer: Distributed SEMS sits between fully centralized and fully decentralized. Devices decide their own set-points like droop control, but they also exchange information occasionally so the controller can adapt. That gives most of the optimisation benefit while keeping resilience if the network goes down.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: by using a decentralized approach in conjunction with a communication capability that is in between individual units and a central server. This is known as distributed architecture.

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How many DC fast chargers does Electrify America operate and with how much battery storage?
qa.answer: Electrify America has installed over 3,500 DC fast chargers spread across roughly 800 sites in the United States. The company has also rolled out about 30 megawatts of battery energy storage at more than 140 of those fast-charging sites to help manage demand.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.evidence_quote: Electrify America, for example, has installed over 3,500 DC fast chargers at 800 sites

qa.question: What does the MING stack stand for in IoT software?
qa.answer: MING is an open-source toolchain for Internet-of-Things applications: MQTT as the messaging broker, InfluxDB as the time-series database, Node-RED as the visual flow tool for transforming data and protocols, and Grafana as the dashboard for visualising the data.

source.book: eCHIP High-Power Charging Hub Design

qa.question: What is CIP.io and where does the eCHIP project plan to run it?
qa.answer: CIP.io is an open-source Internet-of-Things integration platform developed by Argonne in 2016 and built on the MING stack. eCHIP plans to deploy it on a small computer such as an Intel NUC or Dell OptiPlex micro to host the open-source SEMS.

qa.evidence_quote: an IoT Common Integration Platform called CIP.io (pronounced “sip-e-o”), was developed in 2016 by ANL

qa.topic: High-Power EV Charging Hub Design (NREL eCHIP)
qa.question: How does the SEMS dispatch energy when an EV, battery storage, and solar are all on the DC hub?
qa.answer: The rule-based controller serves EV charging power first from solar, then from the battery storage, and only if those are not enough does it pull from the central inverter and the grid. Any surplus solar goes first to the battery, then back out to the grid.

qa.question: Why does an electric-drive vehicle need several separate thermal management systems?
qa.answer: An electric-drive vehicle uses several separate thermal management systems because different subsystems, such as the battery, the motor and the power electronics, all have their own temperature limits. qa.answer: NREL lists three kinds of power electronics as critical inside an EV: inverters, converters and chargers. Together with the electric machines themselves, they steer how power flows from the battery to the wheels and back during regenerative braking events.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 2. Power Electronics and Electric Motor Components

qa.evidence_quote: Power level 100 kW

qa.question: Why does ARPA-E care about electric drives for narrow-body airplanes?
qa.answer: ARPA-E cares about electric drives for narrow-body airplanes because that class typically carries 100 to 200 passengers and is a large share of aviation fuel use. Electrifying it would cut a meaningful chunk of total air-travel emissions if drives can be made light enough.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Single-aisle (narrow-body) airplanes with 100–200 passengers

qa.question: What four physical integration styles does NREL list for combining motor and inverter?
qa.answer: NREL lists four physical integration styles for combining the motor and inverter: separate enclosures, radial integration, axial integration and putting the inverter inside the motor. Each style trades package size against ease of cooling and ease of service.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 3. Integrated Traction Drive and Integration Techniques

qa.evidence_quote:
Separate enclosures
Radial integration
Axial integration
Inside motor

qa.question: Which dielectric fluids does NREL identify as originally meant for battery cooling?
qa.answer: NREL identifies AC100 and Alpha 6 as dielectric fluids that were originally developed for battery thermal management. These non-conductive oils can now also touch motor and inverter parts directly, which is something water-glycol coolants cannot safely do without insulation in between.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Originally for battery thermal management: AC100, Alpha 6

qa.question: What family of dielectric fluids does NREL mention from a major oil brand?
qa.answer: NREL mentions the Mobil EV family of fluids as a commercially available line of dielectric coolants for electric vehicles. Several other formulations from various suppliers are still under development or are proprietary, so the market in EV-specific fluids is still maturing.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Mobil EV family of fluids

qa.question: Why are refrigerant-based two-phase coolers rare in today's automotive power electronics?
qa.answer: Refrigerant-based two-phase coolers are rare in today's automotive power electronics because they are not yet widely adopted. The plumbing and seals needed to handle a boiling and condensing refrigerant safely inside a moving vehicle remain a barrier compared with simpler single-phase liquid loops.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Not yet widely adopted in current automotive power electronics applications

qa.question: Which coolant fluid is used in the first manufacturer configuration NREL reviews where motor and inverter are separate?
qa.answer: In the first manufacturer configuration NREL reviews, the motor and inverter are kept in separate housings and cooled with water and glycol mix. Water-glycol is the standard automotive coolant because it has good heat capacity and resists freezing.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Motor and inverter separate

qa.question: How does the GM Chevy Bolt cool its drive system, according to NREL?
qa.answer: According to NREL, the GM Chevy Bolt keeps its motor and inverter separate or remote and uses a hybrid coolant mix of oil and water-glycol. An integrated oil-to-glycol heat exchanger lets the two loops trade heat with each other inside the drive unit.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Integrated oil-glycol heat exchanger

qa.question: Which Tesla and BMW models does NREL cite when comparing cooling housings?
qa.answer: NREL cites Munro and Associates' 2020 teardown that compares the cooling-strategy housings of the Tesla Model 3 motor against the BMW i3 motor. That source is the basis for several of NREL's manufacturer-by-manufacturer cooling tables in this tutorial.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Comparing the Cooling Strategy Housings of Motors for Tesla Model 3 vs BMW i3

qa.question: Where does NREL show the inverter mounted in the radial-integration configuration on page 16?
qa.answer: In the radial-integration configuration on page 16, NREL shows the inverter radially mounted on one side of the motor. Both the motor stator housing and the inverter share the same water-and-glycol coolant loop, simplifying the plumbing inside the package.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Inverter radial mount on one side of motor

qa.question: How does the cooling configuration on page 17 differ from the one on page 16?
qa.answer: The page 17 configuration also mounts the inverter radially on one side of the motor with water-glycol coolant, but adds air cooling and indirect water-glycol cooling for the rotor end plates plus water-glycol on the rotor shaft. Page 16 has no rotor cooling.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Inverter radial mount on one side of motor

qa.question: What does NREL describe as a WEG heat exchanger on page 18?
qa.answer: On page 18, NREL describes a WEG heat exchanger as a water-ethylene-glycol unit that cools an oil jet. The oil sprays directly onto the stator end windings, then passes its heat into the water-glycol loop through the heat exchanger before recirculating.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Oil jet cooled with water-ethylene- glycol (WEG) heat exchanger

qa.question: Which lubricant does NREL list as the oil-side coolant in the axial-mount configuration on page 19?
qa.answer: In the axial-mount configuration on page 19, NREL lists ATF 9, an automatic transmission fluid, as the oil-side coolant. The oil itself is then cooled by a WEG heat exchanger, so heat ends up rejected to the vehicle's water-glycol radiator loop.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Oil (ATF 9)

qa.question: On NREL's page 20 example, which parts of the motor are cooled by oil?
qa.answer: On NREL's page 20 example, oil cools the stator housing, the stator end winding, the rotor shaft and the inverter itself. The same oil is then sent through a water-ethylene-glycol heat exchanger to reject its heat to the rest of the cooling system.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Oil cooled with WEG heat exchanger

qa.question: How does the page 21 CSI Power Electronics configuration cool its rotor?
qa.answer: On page 21, the CSI Power Electronics configuration uses a radial mount with water-glycol coolant for the stator and inverter, but cools the rotor with air. Air cooling the rotor end plates and shaft avoids sliding seals and complicated rotating coolant joints.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Rotor cooled with air

qa.question: What integrated drive design does NREL highlight from Oak Ridge National Laboratory?
qa.answer: NREL highlights Oak Ridge National Laboratory's outer-rotor motor with an integrated inverter. It uses a single water-ethylene-glycol cooling loop, a cylindrical housing with internal coolant channels for a six-phase inverter, and ceramic heat exchangers embedded between the stator windings.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Outer-Rotor Motor With Integrated Inverter

qa.question: How many T-shape heat exchangers does ORNL use in its stator cooling assembly?
qa.answer: ORNL uses eighteen T-shape heat exchangers in its stator cooling assembly. They are attached to a shared coolant distribution manifold, so a single inlet feeds all of them and the cooling load is spread evenly around the motor stator.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 4. Review of Integration and Cooling Approaches

qa.evidence_quote: Stator cooling assembly with 18 T-shape heat exchangers

qa.question: What kind of inverter housing concept does Bidzina Kekelia present on page 24?
qa.answer: On page 24, Bidzina Kekelia presents a design concept for a cylindrical inverter housing with cooling fluid channels built into the walls. The walls themselves carry the coolant, so the inverter electronics shed heat directly into the housing as they switch.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Design concept of a cylindrical inverter housing with cooling fluid channels in the walls

qa.question: What three sources does NREL list for finding how much heat a motor produces?
qa.answer: NREL lists three sources for finding how much heat a motor produces: the electromagnetic analysis of the design, the MotorCAD design tool and physical testing of a built motor. Designers typically use all three so the answers can be cross-checked.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: From the electromagnetic (EM) analysis From MotorCAD design tool From testing

qa.question: What three temperatures does NREL say a designer must know at the start?
qa.answer: NREL says a designer must know three temperatures at the start of a thermal design: the motor temperature limit, the coolant temperature, and the ambient temperature. Together they bound how much heat the cooling system actually needs to move to keep the motor safe.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Motor temperature limit Coolant temperature Ambient temperature

qa.question: What three cooling methods does NREL list when starting a motor thermal design?
qa.answer: When starting a motor thermal design, NREL lists three cooling methods to choose from: air, liquid and hybrid. Liquid options include water, water-ethylene-glycol mix, engine oil and lubricants, each with its own trade-off between heat capacity and material compatibility.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Air Liquid (water, WEG, engine oil, lubricant…) Hybrid

qa.question: Why does NREL recommend a preliminary estimate before detailed thermal modelling?
qa.answer: NREL recommends a preliminary estimate before detailed thermal modelling because a quick analytical or empirical pass narrows the range of heat transfer coefficients and surface areas. That trims the field of candidate cooling methods early, saving time on detailed simulations that would never work anyway.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Preliminary study is needed before detailed model-based design

qa.question: What two modelling approaches does NREL contrast for thermal management design?
qa.answer: NREL contrasts two modelling approaches for thermal management design: a one-dimensional thermal network that is hard to build but runs very fast, and three-dimensional numerical tools, namely thermal finite element analysis and computational fluid dynamics, which are slower but more detailed.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: 1D thermal network Hard to develop Fast calculation 3D numerical tools

qa.question: What machine specific-power target does NREL list for an aircraft propulsion design?
qa.answer: NREL lists a machine specific-power target of 20 kilowatts per kilogram for an aircraft propulsion design. That figure measures how much output the motor delivers per unit of mass, which matters most when the drive has to be carried into the sky.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: Case Study: Electric System for Commercial

qa.evidence_quote: Machine specific power

qa.question: What peak power does NREL's commercial aircraft case study aim for?
qa.answer: NREL's commercial aircraft case study aims for a peak power of 250 kilowatts and a continuous power of 83 kilowatts. The big gap between the two figures reflects that an aircraft only needs full power briefly during take-off and climb.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: Case Study: Electric System for Commercial

qa.evidence_quote: Peak power

qa.question: What machine efficiency does NREL's aircraft case study require?
qa.answer: NREL's aircraft case study requires a machine efficiency greater than 95 percent. That high efficiency keeps wasted heat low, which directly reduces the size and weight of the cooling system the airplane has to lift along with the motor itself.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: Case Study: Electric System for Commercial

qa.evidence_quote: Machine efficiency

qa.question: Why does NREL only model one-eighteenth of the aircraft electric machine?
qa.answer: NREL only models one-eighteenth of the aircraft electric machine because the design has structural symmetry. Cutting it down to a single repeating slice still captures the physics but shrinks the computational job, so each design iteration runs much faster than it otherwise could.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Due to structural symmetry, 1/18 of electric machine (motor) was extracted for analysis

qa.question: Which loss component is largest inside one slice of the modelled aircraft motor?
qa.answer: The largest loss component inside one slice of the modelled aircraft motor is the AM coil, which dissipates 794 watts. That figure dwarfs the other losses, so cooling that coil well is the first priority in sizing the thermal management system.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.section: Loss Breakdown in 1/18 of the Machine

qa.evidence_quote: AM coil

qa.question: How much heat does the stator core lose in NREL's aircraft motor slice?
qa.answer: In NREL's aircraft motor slice, the stator core loses 277 watts. That is the second-largest contribution, well behind the AM coil. The magnets and friction-and-windage losses come in much smaller, at 36.7 and 68 watts respectively, with heat pipes carrying 94.5 watts.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.section: Loss Breakdown in 1/18 of the Machine

qa.evidence_quote: Stator core

qa.question: Which part of the aircraft motor coil design does NREL describe as critical to characterise?
qa.answer: NREL describes the additively manufactured coil with an embedded heat pipe as the critical part to characterise. The diameter of that heat pipe sets the coil geometry and so the heat losses, which makes the heat pipe a load-bearing design choice.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: additively manufactured coil with embedded heat pipe is the essential part of TMS

qa.question: What two interface materials does NREL test for thermal contact in the heat pipe rig?
qa.answer: NREL tests two interface materials for thermal contact resistance at the heat pipe: thermal paste and a boron nitride coating. Whichever interface is used decides how easily heat crosses from the coil into the heat pipe before the coolant carries it away.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Thermal paste Boron nitride coating

qa.question: What kinds of properties does NREL include in its 3D thermal finite-element model?
