qa.answer: NREL's three-dimensional thermal finite-element model includes potting and insulation layers and material properties such as density and thermal conductivity. It can run as steady-state or transient and can apply uniform or non-uniform heat losses, depending on what the engineer needs to predict.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Include potting and insulation layers Material properties (density, thermal conductivity, etc.)

qa.question: Why does NREL list CFD as more demanding than simple thermal analysis?
qa.answer: NREL lists CFD as more demanding because it takes longer to develop and needs careful knowledge of model parameters and settings. The reward is higher accuracy and location-by-location detail on temperature, heat transfer coefficient and heat flux that simpler tools cannot provide.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: More time to develop Require knowledge for model parameters/setting Higher accuracy

qa.question: How does NREL close the loop between thermal modelling and electromagnetic design?
qa.answer: NREL closes the loop by feeding the temperature predicted in each layer back into the electromagnetic design. The electromagnetic team uses those temperatures to tune the heat losses, and the cycle repeats so cooling and motor design evolve together rather than separately.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Temperature in each layer can be fed back into EM for tuning the heat losses

qa.question: What three goals does NREL list for its electric motor research programme?
qa.answer: NREL's electric motor research programme has three goals: understand material and interface properties as a function of temperature, develop and evaluate advanced fluid-based cooling strategies, and use modelling to guide advanced motor design and development for future drive units.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management
source.chapter: 6. Experimental Characterization of Materials and Coolant HTCs

qa.evidence_quote: Develop and evaluate advanced fluid-based cooling strategies

qa.question: What variable does NREL identify as having a strong influence on the heat transfer coefficient in jet impingement tests?
qa.answer: In NREL's jet impingement tests, the target surface temperature has a strong influence on the heat transfer coefficient. Charts at surface temperatures of 90, 100, 110 and 120 degrees Celsius all show the HTC rising as jet velocity climbs from one to ten metres per second.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Temperature iomf tphaects the HTC

qa.question: What does CTE stand for in NREL's tutorial summary?
qa.answer: In NREL's tutorial summary, CTE stands for Coefficient of Thermal Expansion. A mismatch in CTE between cooling-system components causes them to expand and contract by different amounts when temperatures cycle, leading to stresses, leaks and eventual mechanical failure inside the drive.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) mismatch

qa.question: Why does NREL flag leak-free sealing as a particular challenge for integrated drives?
qa.answer: NREL flags leak-free sealing as a particular challenge because an integrated traction drive packs cooling channels right next to high-voltage electronics. Any leak of coolant inside the unit can short out the inverter, so sealing has to stay perfect through years of thermal cycling.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Leak-free sealing of cooling system components inside an integrated traction drive is challenging

qa.question: Why does NREL conclude that active cooling is critical for future EV traction drives?
qa.answer: NREL concludes that active cooling is critical for future EV traction drives because tomorrow's drives will be even more power-dense than today's. Without fluid moving heat out actively, the small package needed to hit those targets simply cannot stay within safe operating temperatures.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Active cooling is critical for today’s (and especially future) powerdense EV traction drives

qa.question: Which coolant combination does NREL say most manufacturers rely on for EV traction drives?
qa.answer: NREL says most manufacturers rely on a combination of oil and water-glycol fluids for EV traction drives, even though their integration choices differ. Oil cools the high-temperature motor parts directly, then a water-glycol loop takes that heat to the radiator on the outside.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: mostly rely on a combination of oil and water/glycol fluids

qa.question: Why does NREL describe direct driveline fluid cooling as one of the most effective single-phase methods?
qa.answer: NREL describes direct driveline fluid cooling as one of the most effective single-phase methods because the coolant touches hot parts directly rather than going through a housing wall. Cutting out that thermal barrier lets more heat move per square centimetre, so the drive can be smaller.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Direct driveline fluid cooling is potentially one of the most effective (single-phase) thermal management solutions

qa.question: Why does NREL call wider adoption of EV-specific dielectric fluids paramount for future drives?
qa.answer: NREL calls wider adoption of EV-specific dielectric fluids paramount because such fluids let one coolant touch electronics and motor parts directly without a metal barrier. That allows smaller, lighter integrated drive units to hit aggressive volumetric power density targets without overheating.

source.book: Electric Motor and Integrated Traction Drive Thermal Management

qa.evidence_quote: Development and wide adoption of EV-specific dielectric fluids is paramount

qa.question: How many electric cars were sold globally in 2023 and what share of all car sales did that represent?
qa.answer: Almost 14 million new electric cars were registered worldwide in 2023, accounting for about 18% of every car sold that year. The share was up from 14% in 2022 and only 2% in 2018, showing how quickly the market has grown over five years.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Electric cars accounted for around 18% of all cars sold in 2023, up from 14% in 2022 and only 2% 5 years earlier, in 2018.

qa.question: Which three regions accounted for nearly all electric car sales in 2023?
qa.answer: China, Europe and the United States together made up about 95% of global electric car sales in 2023. Roughly 60% of new electric cars were sold in China, just under 25% in Europe and around 10% in the United States, leaving the rest of the world a small share.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: just under 60% of new electric car registrations were in the People’s Republic of China (hereafter “China”), just under 25% in Europe, and 10% in the United States – corresponding to nearly 95% of global electric car sales combined.

qa.question: How many new electric cars were registered in China in 2023?
qa.answer: In China, new electric car registrations reached 8.1 million in 2023, about 35% higher than the previous year. Electric models drove all of China's car-market growth, because sales of conventional petrol and diesel cars actually fell by 8% during the same year.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: In China, the number of new electric car registrations reached 8.1 million in 2023, increasing by 35% relative to 2022.

qa.question: How many electric cars did the United States sell in 2023?
qa.answer: About 1.4 million new electric cars were registered in the United States in 2023, more than 40% higher than in 2022. The Inflation Reduction Act tax credits and price cuts on popular models such as the Tesla Model Y helped drive the increase.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: In the United States, new electric car registrations totalled 1.4 million in 2023, increasing by more than 40% compared to 2022.

qa.question: Which European country had the highest electric car sales share in 2023?
qa.answer: Norway had Europe's highest share, with electric cars making up almost 95% of new car sales in 2023. Norway has long offered strong tax breaks for EVs, and its sales share kept rising even though the country's overall car market shrank that year.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: In Norway, sales shares increased slightly despite the overall market contracting, and its sales share remains the highest in Europe, at almost 95%.

qa.question: Why did Germany's electric car sales share fall from 30% in 2022 to 25% in 2023?
qa.answer: Germany phased out its subsidies for plug-in hybrids at the start of 2023 and ended remaining electric-car subsidies in December 2023 after a court ruling on the Climate and Transformation Fund. Removing this purchase support cooled demand and pulled the overall electric sales share down by five points.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: At the start of 2023, PHEV subsidies were phased out, resulting in lower PHEV sales compared to 2022, and in December 2023, all EV subsidies ended after a ruling on the Climate and Transformation Fund.

qa.question: What is a fuel cell electric vehicle (FCEV), and how large is the global stock?
qa.answer: An FCEV runs on hydrogen, using a fuel cell to make electricity on board. By the end of 2023 there were about 87,600 FCEVs worldwide, with the stock growing roughly 20% in a year. Cars made up half of new sales, with trucks and buses accounting for most of the rest.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the global stock of fuel cell electric vehicles (FCEVs) increased by around 20% compared to 2022, reaching 87 600 by the end of the year.

qa.question: How fast did electric car sales grow in Thailand during 2023?
qa.answer: Thailand's new electric car registrations more than quadrupled in 2023 to nearly 90,000, giving electric models a 10% share of all car sales. New subsidies, lower import and excise taxes, and the arrival of Chinese carmakers all helped drive the surge even as the wider market shrank.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: In Thailand, electric car registrations more than quadrupled year-on-year to nearly 90 000, reaching a notable 10% sales share

qa.question: What share of cars sold in India in 2023 were electric?
qa.answer: About 2% of all new cars sold in India in 2023 were electric, with roughly 80,000 registrations — up 70% from the year before. Subsidies under the FAME II scheme, the PLI manufacturing scheme and new models from Tata, Mahindra and others all helped fuel the growth.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: In India, electric car registrations were up 70% year-on-year to 80 000, compared to a growth rate of under 10% for total car sales. Around 2% of all cars sold were electric.

qa.question: Why has EV adoption been slow in Brazil despite its clean power mix?
qa.answer: Since the late 1970s Brazil has prioritised ethanol-based fuels made from its sugar cane crop, as a way to keep oil imports down. The existing refuelling network and competitive cost of ethanol have given biofuels a head start, slowing the shift to plug-in electric cars in the country.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: EV adoption has been slow thus far, given the national prioritisation of ethanol-based fuels since the late 1970s as a strategy to maintain energy security in the face of oil shocks.

qa.question: How many electric cars were sold globally in the first quarter of 2024?
qa.answer: More than 3 million electric cars were sold worldwide in the first three months of 2024, about 25% more than in the same period of 2023. China alone added roughly half a million extra sales compared with a year earlier, and smaller markets outside the top three grew over 50%.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Electric car sales remained strong in the first quarter of 2024, surpassing those of the same period in 2023 by around 25% to reach more than 3 million.

qa.question: What was the IEA's estimate for total electric car sales in 2024?
qa.answer: The IEA estimated that around 17 million electric cars would be sold in 2024, more than one in five of all cars sold worldwide. This would beat 2023's record by over 20%, with most of the extra sales coming from China and the rest of the world catching up quickly.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: we estimate that electric car sales could reach around 17 million in 2024. This indicates robust growth for a maturing market, with 2024 sales to surpass those of 2023 by more than 20%

qa.question: How many different electric car models were on sale globally in 2023?
qa.answer: Around 590 electric car models were available globally in 2023, about 15% more than in 2022. Two-thirds of them were SUVs, pick-up trucks or other large cars. Based on manufacturer announcements the number could climb to roughly 1,000 different electric models by 2028.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the number of new electric car models could reach 1 000 by 2028

qa.question: Why are carmakers focusing on large electric SUVs rather than small models?
qa.answer: Big vehicles bring carmakers bigger profit margins, which matters because most makers still lose money on EVs. Regulatory rules also favour larger cars: US tax credits allow electric SUVs priced up to USD 80,000 versus USD 55,000 for sedans, and buyers willingly pay more for longer range.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Under the US IRA, electric SUVs can qualify for tax credits as long as they are priced under USD 80 000, whereas the limit stands at USD 55 000 for a sedan, creating an incentive to market SUVs

qa.question: How much critical-mineral demand could be avoided if all 2023 electric SUVs had been medium-sized cars instead?
qa.answer: Replacing every electric SUV sold in 2023 with a medium-sized electric car would have saved around 60 GWh of batteries. That equates to roughly 6,000 tonnes of lithium, 30,000 tonnes of nickel, 7,000 tonnes of cobalt and 8,000 tonnes of manganese — without much loss of driving range.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: around 60 GWh of battery equivalent could have been avoided globally, with limited impact on range. Accounting for the different chemistries used in China, Europe, and the United States, this would be equivalent to almost 6 000 tonnes of lithium, 30 000 tonnes of nickel, almost 7 000 tonnes of cobalt, and over 8 000 tonnes of manganese.

qa.question: How much has the average driving range of battery electric cars improved since 2015?
qa.answer: The sales-weighted average range of battery electric cars grew almost 75% between 2015 and 2023, thanks to bigger batteries and better technology. Small cars stayed near 150 km, while medium cars and SUVs jumped to roughly 380 km, easing worries about long-distance journeys.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the sales-weighted average range of battery electric cars grew by nearly 75% between 2015 and 2023, although trends vary by segment.

qa.question: What is total cost of ownership (TCO) when comparing electric and petrol cars?
qa.answer: Total cost of ownership adds together the upfront purchase price, any subsidies and the running costs over time — fuel or electricity, maintenance, insurance and depreciation. Electric cars usually cost more to buy but less to fuel and service, so TCO captures the full economic comparison.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: lifetime operating costs, such as fuel costs, insurance, maintenance and depreciation, which together make up the total cost of ownership (TCO).

qa.question: What share of electric cars sold in China in 2023 were cheaper than equivalent petrol or diesel cars?
qa.answer: The IEA estimates that more than 60% of the electric cars sold in China in 2023 were already cheaper than the average internal combustion engine equivalent. The share has grown rapidly from under 10% in 2018, helped by fierce price competition and big drops in battery costs.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: We estimate that around 55% of the electric cars sold in China in 2022 were cheaper than their average ICE equivalent, up from under 10% in 2018. Given the further price declines between 2022 and 2023, we estimate that this share increased to around 65% in 2023.

qa.question: How does the US electric car market compare to China's in terms of affordable models?
qa.answer: China sells nearly 50 small electric models priced under CNY 100,000 (USD 15,000). In the United States in 2023 the cheapest electric cars cost around USD 30,000 and only 5% were cheaper than equivalent petrol cars, leaving a wide gap with mass-market combustion options at USD 20,000.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: We estimate that in 2022, only about 5% of the electric cars sold in the United States were cheaper than their average ICE equivalent. In 2023, the cheapest electric cars were priced around USD 30 000

qa.question: How big was the used electric car market in 2023?
qa.answer: The market for second-hand electric cars reached about 800,000 vehicles in China, 400,000 in the United States and over 450,000 across six big European countries. Used EV sales in the US are expected to grow another 40% in 2024 as prices fall and federal rebates apply.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.section: Second-hand markets for electric cars are on the rise

qa.evidence_quote: in 2023, the market size for used electric cars amounted to nearly 800 000 in China, 400 000 in the United States and more than 450 000 for France, Germany, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands and the United Kingdom combined.

qa.question: How many electric two-wheelers and three-wheelers does China sell each year compared with India?
qa.answer: China sold nearly 6 million electric two-wheelers in 2023 — roughly 78% of the global total — while India sold about 880,000 and Southeast Asian nations sold 380,000. For three-wheelers India overtook China in 2023 with over 580,000 sales, helped by FAME II subsidies and tax rebates.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 2. Trends in other light-duty electric vehicles

qa.evidence_quote: China, which accounted for 78% of global sales, with nearly 6 million electric 2Ws sold in 2023, compared to 880 000 in India and 380 000 in ASEAN countries.

qa.question: Why are electric three-wheelers cheaper to own than petrol versions in India?
qa.answer: Even though an electric auto-rickshaw costs about 55% more to buy than a petrol one, fuel and maintenance savings make it more than 50% cheaper to run over an eight-year life. Subsidies and tax rebates help, but the savings still beat petrol by 40% even without them.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 2. Trends in other light-duty electric vehicles

qa.evidence_quote: despite the upfront price being 55% higher than for its gasoline equivalent, the average electric 3W model (auto-rickshaw) is more than 50% cheaper to own after 8 years of service, and even without subsidies is over 40% cheaper

qa.question: How many electric buses were sold worldwide in 2023?
qa.answer: Almost 50,000 electric buses were sold worldwide in 2023, equal to 3% of all bus sales. The global fleet reached around 635,000 buses. China remained dominant with about 60% of sales, while European countries such as Belgium, Norway and Switzerland passed 50% sales shares.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Globally, almost 50 000 electric buses were sold in 2023, equating to 3% of total bus sales and bringing the global stock to approximately 635 000 in total.

qa.question: How fast did electric truck sales grow in 2023?
qa.answer: Global sales of electric trucks rose 35% in 2023 to about 54,000, exceeding electric bus sales for the first time. China led with 70% of these sales, while Europe nearly tripled to over 10,000 and the United States also tripled but only to roughly 1,200 trucks.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Sales of electric trucks increased 35% in 2023 compared to 2022, meaning that total sales of electric trucks surpassed electric buses for the first time, at around 54 000.

qa.question: Why do Chinese makers dominate the choice of electric heavy-duty vehicle models?
qa.answer: Chinese manufacturers produce around 430 different battery-electric heavy-duty vehicle models, far more than the 170 in North America and 120 in Europe. A long head-start in their massive home market for electric buses, plus growing sales abroad, has let them spread offerings across buses, medium and heavy trucks.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Chinese OEMs currently produce by far the largest number of models of battery electric HDVs – 430 in total – with a particular focus on buses suitable for urban public transport

qa.question: How is most electric car charging actually done today?
qa.answer: Most owners charge their cars at home — typically overnight in a private parking space. Home charging is the cheapest and most convenient option, taking advantage of low overnight electricity prices. As a result there are almost ten times as many private chargers worldwide as public ones.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Home charging is currently the most common means of charging electric cars. EV owners with access to a private parking space that can be equipped for charging can charge overnight

qa.question: How quickly did the global public charger network grow in 2023?
qa.answer: The world's public charging stock grew more than 40% in 2023 versus 2022, with fast chargers expanding even faster, at about 55%. By year-end fast chargers made up over 35% of all public chargers, and China alone hosted more than 85% of the world's fast charging points.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: The public charging stock increased by more than 40% in 2023, and the growth of fast chargers – which reached 55% outpaced that of slow chargers.

qa.question: What does the EU's Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation require for highway chargers?
qa.answer: Under the EU's AFIR, member states must install public fast chargers every 60 km along the main Trans-European Transport Network corridors. The rules guarantee at least 1.3 kW of public charging capacity for each battery electric car and another 0.8 kW for each plug-in hybrid registered.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the alternative fuels infrastructure regulation (AFIR), which will require public fast chargers every 60 km along the European Union’s main transport corridors

qa.question: What is the Megawatt Charging System (MCS) for trucks?
qa.answer: The Megawatt Charging System is a standard for very-high-power chargers aimed at electric heavy-duty trucks, delivering up to 3.75 megawatts. That level lets a long-haul truck's huge battery refill quickly enough to compete with diesel fuelling stops, and international bodies have started recognising the standard.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the adoption of the megawatt charging system (MCS) – which allows charging capacity up to 3.75 MW – by international standardisation organisations such as SAE International and the International Organization for Standardization (ISO).

qa.question: What is battery swapping and where is it most widely used?
qa.answer: Battery swapping replaces a depleted pack with a fully charged one at a station, often in under five minutes. It is most developed in China, where as many as half of the electric heavy-duty trucks sold in 2023 were built to support swapping, supported by national and city policies.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Battery swapping can be completed in as little as five minutes, can help to extend battery life through more controlled charging, and can spread power demand over a longer period

qa.question: How much total battery demand from electric vehicles was there in 2023?
qa.answer: Global EV battery demand topped 750 GWh in 2023, about 40% higher than the previous year. Electric cars drove 95% of that growth. China bought roughly 415 GWh, Europe 185 GWh and the United States about 100 GWh, with the rest of the world growing fastest from a small base.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Demand for EV batteries reached more than 750 GWh in 2023, up 40% relative to 2022

qa.question: How much lithium did electric vehicle batteries use in 2023?
qa.answer: Battery demand for lithium reached about 140,000 tonnes in 2023, accounting for 85% of total lithium use and rising more than 30% over 2022. Demand for cobalt from batteries hit 150,000 tonnes (70% of the total), while battery-related nickel demand approached 370,000 tonnes.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Battery demand for lithium stood at around 140 kt in 2023, 85% of total lithium demand and up more than 30% compared to 2022

qa.question: How much did lithium-ion battery pack prices change in 2023?
qa.answer: After a 7% rise in 2022 caused by turmoil in metal markets, lithium-ion battery pack prices fell almost 14% in 2023 as cobalt, graphite and manganese prices dropped. It was the largest annual battery-price decline since 2020, helping bring electric cars closer to price parity.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: This led to an almost 14% fall in battery pack price between 2023 and 2022, despite lithium carbonate prices at the end of 2023 still being about 50% higher than their 2015-2020 average.

qa.question: What is a lithium iron phosphate (LFP) battery and how popular is it?
qa.answer: An LFP battery uses iron and phosphate in its positive electrode instead of nickel and cobalt, making it cheaper and longer-lasting at the cost of slightly less energy per kilogram. LFP supplied more than 40% of EV battery capacity worldwide in 2023, double its share in 2020.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: LFP has moved from a minor share to the rising star of the battery industry, supplying more than 40% of EV demand globally by capacity in 2023, more than double the share recorded in 2020.

qa.question: Why are sodium-ion batteries seen as a promising future option?
qa.answer: Sodium-ion batteries can be made on existing lithium-ion production lines but use no lithium at all, easing pressure on scarce minerals. If scaled up they could cost as much as 20% less than today's lithium batteries, suiting small urban EVs and stationary power storage applications.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: If brought to scale, sodium-ion batteries could cost up to 20% less than incumbent technologies and be suitable for applications such as compact urban EVs and power stationary storage

qa.question: Which two companies led global electric car sales in 2023?
qa.answer: BYD and Tesla led the global electric car market, together accounting for about 35% of all electric car sales in 2023. BYD overtook Tesla as the world's best-selling battery electric carmaker in the second half of the year, and its worldwide sales (BEV plus PHEV) topped 3 million.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 6. Trends in the electric vehicle industry
source.section: Electric vehicle company strategy and market competition

qa.evidence_quote: BYD and Tesla remain far ahead of the curve, together accounting for 35% of all electric car sales in 2023.

qa.question: How much profit per electric car did Tesla make compared with BYD in 2022-2023?
qa.answer: Tesla earned roughly USD 10,000 to USD 15,000 of profit per electric car sold in 2022-2023, mainly thanks to its focus on premium models. BYD made about USD 6,000 per car by selling cheaper models and keeping costs down through in-house battery supply chains.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 6. Trends in the electric vehicle industry
source.section: Electric vehicle company strategy and market competition

qa.evidence_quote: around USD 10 000 to USD 15 000 per electric car sold in 2022-2023 against around USD 6 000 for BYD

qa.question: How many EVs did China export in 2023?
qa.answer: China exported about 1.2 million electric vehicles in 2023, part of a total of over 4 million cars shipped abroad. EV exports rose roughly 80% from 2022, helping China overtake Japan and Germany to become the world's largest car exporter, with Europe and Asia Pacific as main destinations.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: China exported over 4 million cars in 2023, making it the largest auto exporter in the world, among which 1.2 million were EVs.

qa.question: How big was venture capital funding for EV and battery start-ups in 2023?
qa.answer: Early-stage venture capital invested in EV and battery start-ups dropped about 20% to USD 1.4 billion in 2023, while later-stage growth-equity deals fell 35% to USD 10.1 billion. Charging start-ups raised USD 400 million and battery makers USD 260 million in early-stage rounds.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 6. Trends in the electric vehicle industry

qa.evidence_quote: start-ups developing EV and battery technologies dropped 20% to USD 1.4 billion in 2023.

qa.question: What are the three IEA scenarios used to project the future of electric mobility?
qa.answer: The IEA uses three scenarios: the Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS), which counts only laws already on the books; the Announced Pledges Scenario (APS), which assumes all government promises are kept; and the Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario (NZE), a path consistent with limiting warming to 1.5C.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: Stated Policies Scenario (STEPS) and Announced Pledges Scenario (APS) consider historical data through the end of 2023, as well as stated policies and ambitions as of the end of March 2024. The Net Zero Emissions by 2050 Scenario (NZE Scenario)

qa.question: How large is the global electric vehicle fleet expected to be by 2035 under stated policies?
qa.answer: Under the Stated Policies Scenario the global EV stock excluding two- and three-wheelers grows from under 45 million in 2023 to 250 million in 2030 and 525 million in 2035 — meaning more than one in four vehicles on the world's roads would be electric.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the stock of EVs across all modes except for two/three-wheelers (2/3Ws), grows from less than 45 million in 2023 to 250 million in 2030 and reaches 525 million in 2035.

qa.question: What share of new car sales in China could be electric by 2025?
qa.answer: China's Automotive Industry Green and Low-Carbon Development Roadmap 1.0 anticipates that passenger new energy vehicle sales could reach a 50% share as early as 2025, a full decade ahead of the previous 2035 target. Sales then keep rising to about two-thirds in 2030 and 85% in 2035.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: passenger new energy vehicle (NEV) sales could reach a 50% share as soon as 2025, as stated in the recent Automotive Industry Green and Low-Carbon Development Roadmap 1.0

qa.question: How much has been invested in EV and battery manufacturing in 2022 and 2023?
qa.answer: Reported industry announcements from 2022 and 2023 totalled more than USD 275 billion for EV manufacturing and USD 195 billion for batteries, of which roughly USD 190 billion has already been committed. This boom in capital signals strong industry confidence in a rapid electrification path.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: reported investment announcements from 2022 and 2023 alone exceed USD 275 billion in EVs and USD 195 billion in batteries, with around USD 190 billion of the total already committed.

qa.question: How many public chargers are expected globally by 2035?
qa.answer: Under the Announced Pledges Scenario the world will need around 25 million public chargers by 2035 — about six times the four million in service at end-2023. The number could already pass 15 million by 2030 as charging buildouts try to keep pace with rapid EV uptake.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 8. Outlook for electric vehicle charging infrastructure

qa.evidence_quote: the global number of public charging points exceeds 15 million by 2030, up four-fold compared to the almost 4 million operating in 2023. By 2035, this number reaches almost 25 million in the APS, a sixfold increase relative to 2023.

qa.question: Why could daytime charging of electric trucks help integrate solar power?
qa.answer: Solar panels generate the most electricity around midday and afternoon. Charging trucks at loading docks or highway stops during those hours absorbs that solar output, balancing supply and demand and reducing the need to curtail excess solar — turning a potential grid headache into useful flexibility.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 8. Outlook for electric vehicle charging infrastructure

qa.evidence_quote: Daytime charging can more directly support solar PV integration, as loading dock or en-route charging increases demand between midday and the afternoon, when solar PV production is high

qa.question: How much will global EV battery demand grow by 2035?
qa.answer: EV battery demand grows about seven times by 2035 under the Stated Policies Scenario, nine times in the Announced Pledges Scenario, and twelve times in the Net Zero Scenario. In the APS in 2035, weekly battery demand will match a full year's demand back in 2019.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 9. Outlook for battery and energy demand

qa.evidence_quote: In the STEPS, EV battery demand grows fourand-a-half times by 2030, and almost seven times by 2035 compared to 2023. In the APS and the NZE Scenario, demand is significantly higher, multiplied by five and seven times in 2030 and nine and twelve times in 2035

qa.question: How much battery recycling capacity existed in 2023, and how much is planned by 2030?
qa.answer: Global battery recycling capacity reached more than 300 GWh per year in 2023, of which over 80% sat in China. If announced projects all proceed, capacity could exceed 1,500 GWh by 2030, with 70% in China and roughly 10% each in Europe and the United States.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 9. Outlook for battery and energy demand

qa.evidence_quote: Global recycling capacity reached over 300 GWh/year in 2023, of which more than 80% was located in China

qa.question: What share of total electricity demand will EVs use by 2035?
qa.answer: In 2023 electric vehicles took just 0.5% of the world's electricity. By 2035 that share rises to roughly 8% under the Stated Policies Scenario and almost 10% under the Announced Pledges Scenario. The United States and Europe show the largest jumps to around 14-15%.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 9. Outlook for battery and energy demand

qa.evidence_quote: EVs accounted for about 0.5% of the world’s total final electricity consumption in 2023

qa.question: How much oil demand will EVs displace by 2035?
qa.answer: Growing EV fleets will displace about 6 million barrels of oil per day in 2030 and 11-12 million by 2035, with most savings coming from cars, then trucks, buses and two-wheelers. Global demand for oil-based road transport fuels is expected to peak around 2025.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024
source.chapter: 9. Outlook for battery and energy demand

qa.evidence_quote: the projected EV fleet displaces 6 million barrels per day (mb/d) of diesel and gasoline in 2030, a sixfold increase on displacement in 2023. By 2035, even less oil is needed for road transport, with displacement reaching 11 mb/d in the STEPS and 12 mb/d in the APS.

qa.question: How do the lifetime emissions of a battery electric car compare with a petrol car?
qa.answer: A medium-sized battery electric car sold in 2023 emits about half as much greenhouse gas over its life as an equivalent petrol car when production, electricity generation and tailpipe emissions are all counted. By 2035, with cleaner grids, a petrol car emits more than two-and-a-half times as much.

source.book: Global EV Outlook 2024

qa.evidence_quote: the lifecycle emissions of a medium-size battery electric car are about half of those of an equivalent ICEV that is running on oil-based fuels

qa.question: What does AC stand for in EV terminology?
qa.answer: AC stands for alternating current. It refers to a charge of electricity that regularly changes direction along the wire. AC is the most common type of power supplied to homes and businesses, so home outlets generally deliver electricity in this form by default.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: AC (Alternating Current): A charge of electricity that regularly changes direction.

qa.question: What does DC stand for in EV terminology?
qa.answer: DC stands for direct current. It refers to a charge of electricity that flows in just one direction along the wire, without reversing. This is the type of power supplied by a battery, so a vehicle's pack inherently delivers DC.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: DC (Direct Current): A charge

qa.question: Why is a BEV electric motor much more efficient than an internal combustion engine?
qa.answer: BEV electric motors turn about 85 to 90 percent of the electricity they receive into useful rotation at the wheels. An internal combustion engine is only around 25 percent efficient, because most of the fuel energy escapes as wasted heat and noise.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: the electric motors convert 85-90% of the energy into turning the wheels.

qa.question: What is the efficiency of an internal combustion engine vehicle, according to the guide?
qa.answer: An internal combustion engine vehicle is only about 25 percent efficient at using the energy stored in its fuel to move the car. The other 75 percent of the chemical energy in the fuel escapes as wasted heat and noise.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: ICE vehicles are only about 25% efficient at utilizing the stored energy in the fuel

qa.question: How is the energy stored in an EV battery typically measured?
qa.answer: The amount of electric energy stored in an EV battery is normally measured in kilowatt-hours, written as kWh. One kilowatt-hour is the energy used by a one-kilowatt appliance running for an hour, so a larger kWh figure means more driving range.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: The amount of electric energy stored in an EV battery is typically measured in kilowatt-hours.

qa.question: What voltage does a Level 1 EV charger use?
qa.answer: Level 1 charging uses a common household outlet supplying up to 120 volts. This is the same socket type used for everyday appliances, which means the owner can begin charging on day one without installing any dedicated electrical equipment at home.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Charging an EV using a common household outlet up to 120 volts.

qa.question: How long does a Level 1 charger take to fully refill an EV?
qa.answer: Level 1 charging is the slowest method available to an EV owner. Using a regular 120-volt household outlet, it can take roughly 9 to 12 hours or even more to fully recharge a battery electric vehicle from empty to full.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: This is the slowest method of charging and can take up to 9-12 hours

qa.question: What voltage does a Level 2 EV charger use?
qa.answer: A Level 2 charger uses an installed station at 240 volts to top up the battery. That is twice the voltage of a household outlet, which is why most EV manufacturers recommend Level 2 charging to owners for everyday home use.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Charging your EV using an installed charging station at 240 volts.

qa.question: How long does Level 2 charging typically take for a BEV versus a PHEV?
qa.answer: Level 2 charging can fully refill a BEV battery in roughly 5 to 10 hours, depending on the model and the charger output. For a PHEV, which has a much smaller battery, the same Level 2 charger can finish the job in just 2 to 4 hours.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Level 2 charging can fully charge an EV within 5-10 hours for BEVs, and 2-4 hours for PHEVs

qa.question: What is another common name for Level 3 charging?
qa.answer: Level 3 charging is also known as DC fast charging, or DCFC for short. It is the fastest charging method available for an EV because it uses much higher power and higher voltage than a Level 2 station does.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Also known as “DC fast charging” or “DCFC”, this is the fastest method of charging for all EVs

qa.question: How quickly can a Level 3 charger top an EV up to 80 percent?
qa.answer: A Level 3 fast charger can bring an EV battery up to 80 percent capacity in about 30 minutes or less. That makes it the practical option for long road trips when drivers need to recover range quickly during a short stop.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Level 3 charging can charge an EV battery to 80% in about 30 minutes or less.

qa.question: Why are Level 3 charging stations still relatively uncommon?
qa.answer: Level 3 chargers only work with certain types of EVs that support fast charging. They are also more expensive and need much more electrical power than Level 2 stations, so installers tend to deploy them more sparingly along highway corridors.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: they’re more expensive and require more power than Level 2 charging.

qa.question: What is a networked EV charging station?
qa.answer: A networked charging station can talk with other stations and to a server or cloud over a cellular or wireless link. That lets owners report on usage, show real-time charging status, do remote troubleshooting, and accept pay-for-use charging from drivers.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: EV charging stations with the ability to communicate to other stations and/or to a server or the cloud

qa.question: What is a PHEV?
qa.answer: PHEV stands for plug-in hybrid electric vehicle. It is like a regular hybrid but has a larger battery that can be plugged in for recharging. Most PHEVs can drive 25 to 75 kilometres on the battery alone before the engine turns on.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: PHEVs are like HEVs but have larger batteries that can be plugged in for recharging.

qa.question: How does regenerative braking benefit an EV driver?
qa.answer: Regenerative braking slows the moving EV by capturing kinetic energy and storing it back in the battery as electricity. That extends the driving range on each charge and reduces friction-brake wear, leading to less frequent and less costly repair work over time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: regenerative braking extends the EV driving range and reduces brake wear leading to less frequent and less

qa.question: How quickly did EV registrations grow in Canada from 2020 to 2021?
qa.answer: About 74,000 new EVs hit Canadian roads in 2021, making up 3.79 percent of all new vehicle registrations that year. That was a 51 percent increase in registrations compared with 2020, indicating very rapid early adoption of the technology among Canadian drivers.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: EVs made up 3.79% of all new vehicle registrations in 2021, a 51% increase in registrations compared to 2020

qa.question: What is the iZEV federal incentive available at point of sale in Canada?
qa.answer: Through the federal iZEV programme, buyers receive an extra incentive of 2,500 to 5,000 dollars at the point of sale per vehicle. Municipalities can stack this incentive with the provincial EVM rebate, reaching up to about 19,000 dollars total on a passenger vehicle.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: an additional incentive of

qa.question: By how much can switching to an EV cut annual fuel and maintenance costs in Alberta?
qa.answer: In Alberta, operating an EV instead of a conventional vehicle can lower yearly fuel and maintenance costs by up to 72 percent. The exact savings depend on the vehicle type chosen and on the current local cost of fuel and electricity.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: could reduce annual fuel and maintenance costs up to 72%

qa.question: How many fewer moving parts does a typical EV drivetrain have compared with a gasoline car?
qa.answer: A typical EV drivetrain has around 90 percent fewer moving parts than a comparable internal combustion engine drivetrain. That reduces the amount of routine maintenance required and helps keep total ownership costs down for the driver over the life of the vehicle.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Typical EV drivetrains have 90% fewer moving parts

qa.question: How quickly does a Nissan Leaf BEV pay back its extra manufacturing emissions?
qa.answer: A battery electric vehicle typically offsets its excess manufacturing emissions within the first 6 to 16 months of operation. After that, the absence of tailpipe emissions means it keeps emitting far less than an equivalent gasoline car would across its life.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: BEVs typically offset their excess manufacturing emissions within the first 6-16 months of operation

qa.question: What happens to EV batteries when they reach the end of their first life?
qa.answer: When a battery is replaced, it is not dumped in landfill, since that is illegal. Manufacturers repurpose batteries in second-life applications such as renewable energy storage and battery backup systems, where they can serve for roughly another decade before recycling.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: they are not dumped into landfills (it is illegal to do so)

qa.question: How far can a new BEV typically drive on a single charge in Alberta?
qa.answer: According to the guide, new battery electric vehicles can travel up to 400 kilometres or more on a single charge. That comfortably covers the average Albertan commuter's daily needs and is steadily reducing what used to be called range anxiety among drivers.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: New BEVs can travel up to 400+ km on a single charge

qa.question: How much battery capacity does a typical EV lose each year?
qa.answer: A US study found that the average EV battery loses about 1 percent of its cycling capacity per year over a 10-year span. Real-world degradation varies with how the driver uses the vehicle and how often they charge to full each time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide
source.section: 4.2 How long do EV batteries last?

qa.evidence_quote: the average battery cycling capacity loss over a 10-year timeframe was about 1% pe r year

qa.question: When is it considered time to retire an EV battery?
qa.answer: Rather than a fixed timeframe, an EV battery should be retired only when its remaining capacity can no longer cover the driver's typical daily needs. As long as the daily commute fits inside what's left of the pack, the battery still works fine.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide
source.section: 4.2 How long do EV batteries last?

qa.evidence_quote: the retirement of an EV battery should occur when the battery no longer satisfies the daily travel needs of a driver

qa.question: What are some best practices to keep an EV battery healthy?
qa.answer: The guide recommends limiting vehicle use during very high temperatures, avoiding parking in direct sun on hot days, avoiding deep discharges where most of the pack is drained, and avoiding aggressive driving. It also suggests using Level 3 fast charging only when necessary.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Limit the frequency of deep battery discharges where most of the battery capacity is drained

qa.question: Why does an EV need less maintenance than a gasoline car?
qa.answer: An EV has very few moving parts, no oil changes to perform, and no spark plugs to replace. That means much less routine maintenance is needed compared with a conventional vehicle, which translates into real financial savings for the owner over time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide
source.section: 4.3 What kind of maintenance is required?

qa.evidence_quote: There are no oil changes or spark plugs replacements needed.

qa.question: Why do EVs perform well in cold winter weather compared with gasoline vehicles?
qa.answer: An EV has no oil and no combustion engine, so the cold cannot thicken its oil or stop the engine from turning over. The battery can still start the vehicle on the coldest days, and resistance heating warms the cabin almost instantly without idling.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide
source.section: 4.4 Do they work in the winter?

qa.evidence_quote: With an EV, there is no oil or combustion engine

qa.question: How much can cold-weather heating reduce an EV's battery range?
qa.answer: Running resistance heating warms the cabin almost immediately, but it does consume battery energy. The guide notes that heating use can draw down the pack by as much as 30 to 40 percent in some cases, although the typical daily commute is still manageable.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide
source.section: 4.4 Do they work in the winter?

qa.evidence_quote: in some cases, upwards of 30 to 40%

qa.question: What share of new cars sold in Norway in March 2019 were fully electric?
qa.answer: In March 2019, almost 60 percent of all new cars sold in Norway were fully electric. The guide uses this fact as evidence that EV adoption can succeed in cold winter climates similar to Alberta's, where ICE vehicles sometimes struggle to start.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide
source.section: 4.4 Do they work in the winter?

qa.evidence_quote: almost 60% of all new cars sold in Norway

qa.question: How can EV drivers maximize their cold-weather range?
qa.answer: Drivers can keep the EV stored in a heated garage, plug it in when not in use, limit the cabin heater while driving, and switch to eco-mode to adjust performance parameters. These small habits help preserve battery energy for actual driving.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Keep your EV stored in a garage (ideally heated)

qa.question: By how much can an EV cut fleet greenhouse gas emissions on Alberta's grid?
qa.answer: A Simon Fraser University study found that charging an EV on Alberta's current electricity grid can lower fleet average greenhouse gas emissions intensity by 41 percent. As more renewables are added to the grid, that reduction is expected to grow further over time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: charging an EV on Alberta’ s electricity grid can reduce fleet average GHG emissions intensity by 41%

qa.question: How does a 2022 Hyundai Kona EV compare with a Tucson ICEV in emissions?
qa.answer: Using 2019 Alberta grid carbon intensity, a 2022 Hyundai Kona EV emits roughly 40 percent less greenhouse gas than a comparable Tucson gasoline SUV over the same 10,000 kilometres. As the grid gets cleaner, the EV advantage is projected to grow steadily.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: the Kona EV emits roughly 40%

qa.question: What share of battery manufacturing emissions comes from electricity use?
qa.answer: Around half of the greenhouse gas emissions associated with making a lithium-ion battery comes from the electricity used during production. As more renewable energy is added to the grid powering battery factories, those associated emissions will continue to decrease over time.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: around half of the associated GHG emissions of battery manufacturing comes from the electricity used

qa.question: Why is lifecycle analysis important when comparing EVs and ICE vehicles?
qa.answer: Manufacturing the battery for an EV releases more CO2 than building a gasoline car, but ICE vehicles emit far more during use due to constant tailpipe pollution. Looking at the full lifecycle lets a fair comparison emerge across both manufacturing and operating emissions.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Lifecycle greenhouse gas emission for a conventional ICE vehicle

qa.question: What does the Level 1 charger physically connect to in a home?
qa.answer: A Level 1 charger is a regular 120-volt household plug, used with an adapter to connect to an electric vehicle. The Level 1 cordset typically comes bundled with the purchase of a passenger EV, so the owner has it ready on delivery day.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: A Level 1 charger is a regular 120-volt household plug that utilizes an adapter to charge an electric vehicle.

qa.question: What standard connector does a Level 2 charger use in Canada and the US?
qa.answer: Level 2 charging stations use the SAE J1772 plug, the standard adopted by Canadian and American EV manufacturers for cross-compatibility. Because nearly every vehicle on the road in those countries supports it, public Level 2 stations typically share this same plug type.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: a standard connector (SAE J1772 plug) adopted by Canadian and American electric vehicle manufacturers

qa.question: What household appliance shares the same outlet voltage as a Level 2 EV charger?
qa.answer: A Level 2 charging station plugs into the same 240-volt outlet used by a clothes dryer or an electric oven. Because most homes already have that circuit available, installation is often straightforward and avoids any major electrical panel rework for the homeowner.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: plug into the same 240V outlet that a clothes dryer or oven would use

qa.question: What two plug types do Level 3 chargers commonly use?
qa.answer: Level 3 chargers use the CHAdeMO connector and the SAE Combo CCS, which stands for combined charging system. Older EVs may not be able to use them at all, but fast-charging capability is rapidly becoming standard equipment on newer EV models.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: This type of charger utilizes the CHAdeMO and SAE Combo CCS

qa.question: Why should EV drivers check charging-station compatibility before driving to one?
qa.answer: Not every vehicle works with every charger type. For example, the Chevrolet Volt, a plug-in hybrid, is not compatible with Level 3 fast charging stations. Checking ahead avoids arriving at a station only to find that the vehicle cannot use it.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Some vehicles, like the Chevrolet Volt (a PHEV), are not compatible with Level 3 stations.

qa.question: What is OCPP and why does it matter for EV charging stations?
qa.answer: OCPP stands for Open Charge Point Protocol, an open syntax language for talking between networked stations and management systems. Choosing an OCPP-compliant station lets the owner freely switch networks later, avoiding being locked into one vendor's fees and infrastructure.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Open Charge Point Protocol or OCPP is a syntax language that is used to communicate with other networked charging stations

qa.question: Where do fast charging stations make the most operational sense?
qa.answer: Fast chargers fit best at sites where the vehicle will only be parked for about 30 minutes, such as highway rest stops and downtown cafes. Level 2 stations suit locations where drivers will linger for hours, like shopping malls, gyms, or parks.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: locations near highway rest stops, downtown cafes, etc.

qa.question: What is the typical Level 2 charge time for a 2022 Nissan Leaf S Plus?
qa.answer: According to the guide's BEV table, the 2022 Nissan Leaf S Plus has a 62 kilowatt-hour battery and takes about 11 hours to fully charge from empty using a Level 2 station. Its driving range is roughly 363 kilometres on a full charge.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.question: What is the electric-only range of a 2022 Hyundai Tucson PHEV?
qa.answer: The 2022 Hyundai Tucson PHEV has a 13.8 kilowatt-hour battery and can travel about 53 kilometres on the electric motor alone. After that, the gasoline engine kicks in, with a typical MSRP listed in the guide of around 30,454 Canadian dollars.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.question: What range does a 2022 Toyota RAV4 Prime PHEV achieve on electricity alone?
qa.answer: The 2022 Toyota RAV4 Prime PHEV has an 18 kilowatt-hour battery and an electric-only range of about 68 kilometres before the gasoline engine kicks in. A Level 2 charger refills its pack from empty in roughly 4.5 hours, ready for the next trip.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.question: What types of heavy-duty electric vehicles are listed in the guide?
qa.answer: The guide lists electric cargo vans, cargo trucks, refuse or garbage trucks, and electric work trucks among medium and heavy-duty options for fleet use. Models from Ford, Lion Electric, Mitsubishi Fuso, BYD, Mack, and Motiv Power Systems are included in the table.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Cargo VanFordE Transit

qa.question: What non-road electric vehicle types are commonly available for fleets?
qa.answer: The guide includes electric tractors, golf carts, off-road work vehicles, parkade sweepers, riding mowers, skid steers, street sweepers, utility task vehicles, and even snow removal trucks. Brands such as Polaris, Bobcat, Greenworks, Tennant, and John Deere all appear in the list.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Electric TractorSoletrac

qa.question: What snow removal vehicle brand appears in the EV guide's table?
qa.answer: The guide lists Claes Equipment for snow removal vehicles, with models including a multi-function truck, a BR rotary broom, an FBBhr, and a TBTT. Pricing for each model is not listed, so prospective buyers must contact the provider directly for quotes.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Snow Removal VehicleClaes Equipment

qa.question: What does the iZEV program provide and to whom?
qa.answer: Launched in May 2019, the federal iZEV programme gives a point-of-sale rebate of 5,000 dollars for BEVs and longer-range plug-in hybrids, and 2,500 dollars for shorter-range PHEVs. It is open to consumers, businesses, and provincial or municipal governments.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Rebates for BEVs, and longer-range plug-in hybrid vehicles are eligible for a

qa.question: What is the ZEVIP and what kind of charging does it fund?
qa.answer: The Zero-Emission Vehicle Infrastructure Program is a Canadian federal scheme to build reliable EV infrastructure with Level 2 or higher charging stations. It funds up to 50 percent of project costs, up to a maximum of 5 million dollars per project.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: Level 2 or higher charging stations to coincide with future transportation goals

qa.question: What are British Columbia's Zero-Emission Vehicles Act sales targets?
qa.answer: Under British Columbia's Zero-Emission Vehicles Act, automakers must reach 10 percent zero-emission light-duty vehicle sales by 2025, 30 percent by 2030, and 100 percent by 2040. BC was the first jurisdiction to legislate a 100 percent ZEV target by law.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Knowledge Guide

qa.evidence_quote: 10% of light-duty vehicle sales by 2025, 30% by 2030, and 100% by 2040

qa.question: What does the term 'EV' cover according to the US DOE?
qa.answer: The label EV is used by the DOE for vehicles that run on electricity as their primary fuel or to improve efficiency. It includes all-electric vehicles, also called battery electric vehicles or BEVs, and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles, called PHEVs.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: EVs include all-electric vehicles, also referred to as battery electric vehicles (BEVs), and plug-in hybrid electric vehicles (PHEVs).

qa.question: What driving feel do EVs offer compared with gasoline cars?
qa.answer: EVs are known for delivering instant torque the moment the driver presses the pedal, plus a quiet ride. There is no engine roaring under the hood, which makes the cabin much calmer than in a typical gasoline-powered vehicle on the road.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: EVs are known for providing instant torque and a quiet driver experience.

qa.question: How do fuel cell electric vehicles differ from battery electric vehicles?
qa.answer: Fuel cell electric vehicles use a propulsion system similar to BEVs, but the onboard energy is stored as hydrogen rather than in a battery. A fuel cell then converts that hydrogen into electricity to drive the motor, instead of drawing from a plug-charged pack.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: fuel cell electric vehicles, which use a propulsion system similar to electric vehicles, where energy stored as hydrogen is converted to electricity by the fuel cell.

qa.question: How are batteries in all-electric vehicles recharged?
qa.answer: Batteries in all-electric vehicles are recharged by plugging the vehicle into an outside electric power source. They can also be topped up by regenerative braking, which captures energy that would otherwise be wasted as heat in the brake hardware itself.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: an electric power source and can also be charged through regenerative braking.

qa.question: Do all-electric vehicles produce tailpipe emissions while driving?
qa.answer: No, an all-electric vehicle has no exhaust pipe and emits nothing at the wheels itself. There are still life-cycle emissions tied to producing the electricity that charges it, but the vehicle itself emits zero pollutants directly during normal use on the road.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: All-electric vehicles produce no tailpipe emissions, although there are “life cycle” emissions associated with the electricity production.

qa.question: What driving range do most new BEVs offer on a single charge?
qa.answer: Most new battery electric vehicles are designed to travel between 110 and over 300 miles on a full charge, depending on the specific model chosen. Actual range varies with how the car is driven and the weather conditions during the trip itself.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Most new BEVs are designed to travel between 110 and over 300 miles on a fully charged battery, depending on the model.

qa.question: Why is the 100-mile reference distance for EV range significant for US drivers?
qa.answer: Roughly 90 percent of all US household trips cover less than 100 miles in a single journey. So a BEV with a 110-plus-mile range can comfortably handle the vast majority of everyday driving without any worry about recharging mid-trip on the road.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: 90% of all U.S. household trips cover less than 100 miles.

qa.question: Why do extreme temperatures tend to reduce an EV's range?
qa.answer: In very hot or cold weather the battery has to power the cabin climate-control system as well as the drive motor. Heating or cooling the cabin draws extra energy from the same pack, which means fewer kilowatt-hours are left to push the car forward.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Extreme temperatures tend to reduce range because energy from the battery powers climate control systems in addition to powering the motor.

qa.question: Which driving habits can reduce an EV's range?
qa.answer: Speeding, aggressive acceleration, and carrying heavy loads can all cut the distance an EV achieves between charges on the road. These behaviours force the motor to draw more current from the battery, draining the pack faster than gentler driving would do.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Speeding, aggressive driving, and heavy loads can also reduce range.

qa.question: What does regenerative braking do on an electric vehicle?
qa.answer: Regenerative braking lets the EV capture energy normally lost when slowing down to a stop. It does this by running the electric motor in reverse as a generator and feeding the recovered electricity back into the battery to extend the driving range.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Regenerative braking allows EVs to capture energy normally lost during braking by using the electric motor as a generator and storing that captured energy in the battery.

qa.question: How is a PHEV powered?
qa.answer: A plug-in hybrid uses a battery to drive an electric motor and also carries a conventional engine that burns another fuel, usually gasoline. The car can run on stored electricity for short trips and then switch to the engine when the battery runs low.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: PHEVs use batteries to power an electric motor and use another fuel, such as gasoline, to power a conventional engine.

qa.question: What is the all-electric driving range of a typical PHEV?
qa.answer: A typical plug-in hybrid can travel roughly 15 to 60 miles or more on the battery alone, depending on the specific model chosen. After that, the conventional engine kicks in to provide extra range using the liquid fuel stored in the tank.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: PHEVs have an all-electric driving range of about 15 to 60+ miles, depending on the model.

qa.question: Can a PHEV be driven without ever being plugged in?
qa.answer: Yes, a plug-in hybrid does not have to be plugged in before being driven and can run solely on conventional liquid fuel. However, skipping the charging step means the driver will not get maximum fuel economy or the full electric-driving benefit.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: PHEVs don’t have to be plugged in before driving. They can also be fueled solely with conventional fuel.

qa.question: When does the engine in a PHEV typically switch on?
qa.answer: The engine turns on when the battery is mostly depleted, during rapid acceleration, at high cruising speeds, or when the cabin needs intense heating or air conditioning. Otherwise the car prefers to use the electric motor for typical daily driving.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: The engine will then power on when the battery is mostly depleted, during rapid acceleration, at high speeds, or when intensive heating or air conditioning is required.

qa.question: Do PHEVs emit pollutants when running purely on the battery?
qa.answer: No, when a plug-in hybrid is drawing only on battery power, no tailpipe pollutants come out at all. Even once the gasoline engine kicks in, PHEVs typically burn less fuel and emit less than a comparable conventional car on the road.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: When running on battery power alone, PHEVs produce no tailpipe emissions.

qa.question: How many publicly available charging outlets exist across the United States?
qa.answer: The DOE notes there are more than 140,000 publicly available charging outlets across the country at present, and the network continues to grow. That widening network helps ease the worry of running out of charge on longer drives.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: There are more than 140,000 publicly available charging outlets— and counting— across the country.

qa.question: What is 'range anxiety' and how is it being addressed?
qa.answer: Range anxiety is the fear of running out of charge before reaching a destination. Larger batteries on newer EVs and a fast-growing public charging network are steadily reducing this worry by giving drivers more miles per charge and more places to refill.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: Technological advances such as larger batteries and growing access to charging are increasingly addressing “range anxiety,” or the fear of running out of charge.

qa.question: What is the minimum time it can take to fully charge an EV battery?
qa.answer: Under the right conditions a battery can be topped up in as little as 30 minutes from empty. The exact time depends on the charger type, the size and state of the battery, and the rating of the vehicle's onboard charger hardware.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: It may take as little as 30 minutes to fully charge an EV’s battery, but charging times vary based on the type or level of charging

qa.question: What kind of cordset is included with most new EVs?
qa.answer: Most EVs come with a 110-volt Level 1 cordset that simply plugs into a typical wall outlet at home. This basic cable allows the owner to begin charging on day one without needing to install any new electrical equipment in the house.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: Most EVs come with a 110-volt “Level 1” cordset that can be plugged into a typical electrical outlet.

qa.question: Why is installing a Level 2 charger at home often straightforward?
qa.answer: Most American homes already have a 240-volt service available for appliances such as electric dryers and ranges. A Level 2 charging unit taps into that same circuit, so the upgrade often needs little or no extra electrical work to set up.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: This can often be done with little or no required electrical upgrades, as most homes have 240-V service for appliances like dryers and electric ranges.

qa.question: What is the lowest possible purchase price the DOE quotes for a Level 2 unit?
qa.answer: A Level 2 charging unit can be purchased for as little as 200 US dollars, according to the DOE document. It can be wall-mounted permanently in a building or kept portable, depending on the homeowner's preference and the home electrical setup.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: A Level 2 unit can be portable or “hard wired” into a building

qa.question: What are designated EV corridors in the United States?
qa.answer: These are stretches of highway where DC fast charging stations are being installed at regular intervals along the route. The Federal Highway Administration is setting up this network so drivers can complete long trips by topping up quickly along the way.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Do These Vehicles Fuel Up?

qa.evidence_quote: Designated EV corridors2 supported by DC fast charging stations are under development nationwide.

qa.question: Why do EV owners typically charge at home rather than at public stations?
qa.answer: Home charging is generally more convenient and more cost-effective than using public stations on the road. Most EV owners therefore plug in overnight at home or during the workday at the office, treating public chargers mostly as a backup for trips.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Most EV owners charge at home or at work because charging is more convenient and cost-effective than using public stations.

qa.question: Why are electricity prices considered an advantage of going electric?
qa.answer: Electricity prices are less volatile than gasoline and diesel prices, which can swing up and down with oil markets. That stability lets EV drivers forecast their fuelling expenses month to month with much more confidence than gasoline car owners can.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: electricity prices are less volatile than gasoline and diesel prices, so drivers can more easily forecast their fueling expenses.

qa.question: What charging rate does a Level 1 station typically deliver?
qa.answer: A Level 1 station typically adds about 2 to 5 miles of driving range for every hour of charging the vehicle. That is slow, so it suits overnight top-ups for short daily commutes rather than recovering range quickly during the day.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: miles of range per hour of charging

qa.question: How much range does a Level 2 charger typically add per hour?
qa.answer: A Level 2 station adds roughly 10 to 30 miles of driving range every hour that the vehicle is plugged in. That makes it well suited for overnight home charging or a few hours plugged in at work, comfortably covering most daily commutes.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Charging Options

qa.question: What does a DC fast charging station deliver in 30 minutes?
qa.answer: A DC fast charging station can add 100 to over 200 miles of driving range in roughly 30 minutes of plugged-in time. That makes it the practical option for highway road trips when drivers need to recover range quickly during a short stop.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: 100-200+ miles of range in 30 minutes

qa.question: How does the equipment cost of a Level 1 setup compare with a DC fast charging unit?
qa.answer: A Level 1 setup is essentially free to as much as 1,800 US dollars to install at a site. A DC fast charging unit, by contrast, runs from 30,000 to over 120,000 dollars, because higher power ratings demand much more expensive hardware and wiring.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Costs do not include the price of operation

qa.question: What is the typical power-output range of a Level 2 unit?
qa.answer: A Level 2 charging unit usually operates somewhere between 6.6 and 19.2 kilowatts of output power at the plug. The higher its power rating, the faster it charges the car and the more expensive the unit and its wiring tend to be.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: A Level 2 unit can range from 6.6 to 19.2 kW output.

qa.question: What is the power range of a DC fast charging unit?
qa.answer: A DC fast charging unit can range from 25 kilowatts at the low end to 350 kilowatts at the high end of the scale. More powerful units add miles to the battery faster but also cost much more to buy and to install.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: A DCFC unit can range from 25 to 350 kW.

qa.question: Why does the onboard equipment matter for Level 1 and Level 2 charging?
qa.answer: Both Level 1 and Level 2 chargers deliver alternating current, but EV batteries store direct current internally. The vehicle's onboard charger therefore has to convert the incoming AC into DC before it can be used to refill the battery itself.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: vehicle’s onboard equipment converting AC to DC to charge the batteries

qa.question: What makes DC fast charging faster than Level 1 or Level 2?
qa.answer: DC fast chargers send direct current straight into the battery, skipping the conversion step normally done by the car's onboard charger. With more raw power flowing in, the pack refills much faster than it would using AC charging alone.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: DC fast charging (DCFC)— provides DC power directly.

qa.question: What is wireless or inductive EV charging?
qa.answer: Wireless charging equipment uses an electromagnetic field to transfer electricity from a pad to the vehicle without any cable. It is mostly used today at higher power levels for heavy-duty applications such as transit buses parked over an in-ground pad.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Wireless or “inductive” charging equipment uses an electromagnetic field to transfer electricity to an EV without a cord.

qa.question: Why are EVs described as more than three times as efficient as gasoline cars?
qa.answer: Electric drivetrains turn a far larger share of their stored energy into actual motion at the wheels than internal-combustion engines do. The DOE notes that BEVs and PHEVs running in electric mode are more than three times as efficient as conventional vehicles.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Much Do the Vehicles Cost?

qa.evidence_quote: electric drivetrains are more efficient, making BEVs (and PHEVs operating in electric mode) more than three times as efficient.

qa.question: How much can a BEV driver save on fuel over 15 years?
qa.answer: The DOE estimates that a battery electric vehicle driver can save as much as 14,500 US dollars on fuel costs alone over a 15-year ownership period. That assumes electricity remains cheaper than gasoline on an energy-equivalent basis throughout the time owned.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics
source.section: How Much Do the Vehicles Cost?

qa.evidence_quote: BEV drivers can save as much as

qa.question: How do tailpipe emissions of all-electric vehicles compare to conventional cars?
qa.answer: All-electric vehicles produce zero tailpipe emissions whenever they are running on battery power alone. Conventional cars, by contrast, emit a steady stream of pollutants from the exhaust whenever the engine is operating, because they simply do not have an exhaust pipe.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: All-electric vehicles produce zero tailpipe emissions

qa.question: What life-cycle emissions are tied to an EV?
qa.answer: Life-cycle emissions for an EV include those from generating the electricity that charges it and from manufacturing the vehicle itself. The amount depends largely on how clean the regional power grid is and how much a PHEV's engine is used over its life.

source.book: Electric Vehicle Basics

qa.evidence_quote: Life cycle emissions are generated when fuel or electricity is produced, as well as during the manufacturing of the vehicle itself.

qa.question: Roughly what share of conventional vehicle emissions do EVs and PHEVs produce?
qa.answer: Generally all-electric vehicles produce about one-third of the emissions of a conventional car, and PHEVs about half, when both air pollutants and greenhouse gases are counted across the full life cycle of fuel and electricity used by each one.
